Separately, a person familiar with the matter told Breakingviews that Novo bosses spent two days last week discussing the future, including M&A, at a management meeting. Big changes may be coming. ‚ÄãThe fact that they're necessary testifies to how much Novo has scuppered its lead.
Follow Aimee Donnellan on LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Why Africa‚Äôs growth prospects may be looking up: podcast
item.description:
Follow on Apple or Spotify . Listen on the Reuters app.
It‚Äôs easy to be glum about a continent grappling with US tariffs, cuts to aid, and climate change. Yet Joe Studwell, author of ‚ÄòHow Africa Works‚Äô, says the conditions for development are in place. In this episode of The Big View, he tells Peter Thal Larsen why he‚Äôs optimistic.
Follow Peter Thal Larsen on Bluesky and LinkedIn .
(The host is a Reuters Breakingviews columnist. The opinions expressed are his own.)

Shifting world order suits ‚Äòinbetweener‚Äô economies

item.title: Netanyahu says US-Israel war on Iran 'not going to take years'
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said he expected the war against Iran was "not going to take years", as the conflict widened with Israel attacking Iran-backed Hezbollah targets in Lebanon and Iran hitting Gulf states that host U.S. bases.
U.S. President Donald Trump initially projected the war to last four ‚Äãto five weeks, but since sought to justify a broad, open-ended war on Iran .
Netanyahu rejected the idea of the conflict lasting years, like previous wars in the region.
"I said it could be quick ‚Äåand decisive. It may take some time, but it's not going to take years. It's not an endless war," Netanyahu said on Fox News' "Hannity" program on Monday.
As the war entered its fourth day on Tuesday, explosions shook buildings across Tel Aviv as air defenses intercepted incoming Iranian missiles.
Israel attacked the complex that houses Iran's state broadcaster IRIB in Tehran and targeted Hezbollah militants in towns across Lebanon.
Early on Tuesday, two drones, apparently from Iran, struck the U.S. embassy in Riyadh , causing minor damage and starting a fire, and at least eight more drones were intercepted before ‚Äãreaching the city, Saudi Arabia's Defense Ministry said.
The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said on Tuesday that its naval forces had destroyed the main command building and headquarters of a U.S. airbase in Bahrain in what ‚Äãit described as the 14th wave of ‚ÄúOperation Promise of the Truth 4‚Äù.
The IRGC said in a statement that it had launched a large-scale drone and missile attack on the base ‚ÅÝin the Sheikh Isa area early in the morning, with 20 drones and three missiles striking their intended targets.
The U.S. State Department and the White House did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio ‚Äãon Monday warned that ‚Äúthe hardest hits are yet to come from the U.S. military‚Äù in the offensive against Iran.
Asked how long he expected the United States to be engaged in Iran, Rubio told reporters that he did not know, and ‚Äãthat he did not rule out the possibility that Trump might deploy U.S. troops to fight a ground war in the Middle East.
"We believe the objectives we have set for this mission, the destruction of their ballistic missile capabilities, both launch capibilities and manufacturing can be achieved without ground forces," Rubio said.
"Right now we are not postured for ground forces. But obviously the president has those options and he is not going to rule out anything."
The U.S. and Israeli air war against Iran began with attacks against Tehran on Saturday, which killed ‚ÄãIranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. Retaliation from Iran and its proxy Hezbollah has dragged the wider Gulf region into the conflict, killing hundreds of civilians in Iran, Israel and Lebanon.
The U.S. military said it had struck more than 1,250 targets ‚Äãin Iran and destroyed 11 Iranian ships. Six U.S. service personnel have been killed so far, all in Iran's retaliatory attacks over the weekend on Kuwait.
Kuwait mistakenly shot down three American F-15E fighter jets during an Iranian attack, U.S. Central Command said. All six ‚Äåcrew members ejected ‚ÅÝand were safely recovered.
The conflict has thrown global air transport into chaos and shut down shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, where one-fifth of the world's oil trade skirts the Iranian coast, sending oil prices surging .
Major Gulf hubs, including the world's busiest international airport Dubai, which usually handles over 1,000 flights a day, remained closed for a fourth day due to the conflict. That has left tens of thousands of passengers stranded as aviation faced its biggest test since the COVID-19 pandemic.
Asian airline shares extended losses on Tuesday, with carriers closely monitoring fuel price spikes and many seeing a surge in bookings as passengers switch from Middle Eastern airlines.
Global oil and gas shipping rates soared, with supertanker costs in the Middle East hitting all-time highs, after ‚ÄãTehran targeted ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz, according ‚Äãto shipping data and industry sources on Tuesday.
Trump has said the U.S. faced an imminent threat from Iran that justified the war, although he gave no specifics and some U.S. lawmakers said he has shown no evidence to back that assessment.
U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio told reporters on Monday that the United States acted preemptively because it knew of close ally Israel's plan to strike ‚ÄãIran and knew Tehran would respond, putting U.S. bases at risk.
"We knew that that would precipitate an attack against American forces, and we knew that if we didn't ‚Äãpreemptively go after them before they ‚ÅÝlaunched those attacks, we would suffer higher casualties," Rubio said.
In his most extensive public comments so far on the conflict, Trump on Monday said he had ordered the attack to thwart Tehran's nuclear program and a ballistic missile program that he said was growing rapidly.
Commercial satellite imagery has captured what appears to be the first known strikes on an Iranian nuclear site since the start of the war, an independent policy institute said on Monday.
Iran has denied it is seeking nuclear weapons and said the U.S. and ‚ÅÝIsraeli assault was ‚Äãunprovoked, occurring as Tehran and Washington were in negotiations on a nuclear accord.
Trump withdrew from a prior international agreement curbing Iran's nuclear program during ‚Äãhis first term in 2018, three years after it was signed.
Trump's assault on Iran is the biggest U.S. foreign policy gamble in decades and a major political risk for his Republican Party in this year's midterm elections, with only one in four Americans saying they support the Iran attack, according to a ‚Äãweekend Reuters/Ipsos poll.
Russia, China and Turkey have condemned the war.

item.title: At least 42 civilians killed in Afghanistan in conflict with Pakistan, UN agency says
item.description: KABUL, March 3 (Reuters) - At ‚Äãleast 42 ‚Äåcivilians have been killed ‚Äãand ‚Äã104 wounded in ‚ÅÝAfghanistan ‚Äãin the ‚Äãfighting with Pakistan between February ‚Äã26 ‚Äãand March 2, ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝUnited Nations Assistance Mission ‚Äãin ‚ÄãAfghanistan ‚ÅÝsaid on Tuesday.

item.title: Europe's far right unites around killing of French activist
item.description:
PARIS/BERLIN, March 3 (Reuters) - The killing of a young activist in France - cast by some as the country's "Charlie Kirk moment" - has galvanised Europe's far right, sparking coordinated rallies across the continent that highlight how local groups are building cross-border networks.
Quentin Deranque, ‚Äãa 23-year-old far-right activist, died after being beaten during a fight with far-left activists in Lyon on February 14, drawing comparisons with last year's shooting of the U.S. conservative activist Kirk .
In the days ‚Äåthat followed, far-right groups crossed borders to march in Lyon and held memorials in at least two dozen European cities, including Rome, Dresden and Zagreb.
"His death must awaken a European-wide movement," said Martin Sellner, an Austrian star of the far right who held a vigil in Vienna, in a video posted on X.
Around 3,000 people attended the Lyon march a week after the killing, with participants raising their arms in Nazi salutes and shouting racist and homophobic slurs.
Among the groups present were Germany's Identitarian movement, classed by that country's ‚Äãdomestic intelligence as an extremist organisation with some ties to the Alternative for Germany (AfD) party. Also present, according to their Telegram channels, were CasaPound and Lealta Azione - Italian groups with roots in neo-fascist ideology.
A French ‚Äãgovernment source confirmed the presence of Italian, Swiss and German far-right groups, without specifying which ones.
Few European governments provide data on far-right group membership. Germany, which closely monitors ‚ÅÝsuch groups because of its Nazi past, estimated 50,520 right-wing extremists in 2024, up 50% from 2020.
A 2023 French parliamentary report said international collaboration was mostly exchanging ideology and did not translate into "operational links" between ‚Äãgroups.
But that has now changed, said Eric Poulliat, the report's author and a former member of parliament. "It is an evolution."
The shift is also recognised outside France.
"The far right is actually quite successful in forming international bonds," said Lorenz Blumenthaler, ‚Äãa Berlin-based analyst with the Amadeu Antonio Foundation, adding that Deranque's killing allowed groups "to mobilise even further" on social media.
Even as governments warn of the threat posed by the far right, the movement is no longer shunned in mainstream politics. Policies such as "remigration" ‚Äì the expulsion of non-white immigrants and their descendants ‚Äì have moved from fringe discussion into the rhetoric of parties like Germany's AfD as well as the Trump administration.
Britain's right-wing Reform UK has said it would carry out mass deportations and abolish the main system for immigrants to become ‚Äãcitizens. France's National Rally (RN) advocates for "national preference" policies and wants to remove residency from unemployed immigrants and restrict family reunification.
Lyon authorities were concerned about activists travelling from across France and Europe for the march, and stationed police ‚Äãat city entry points, said a source close to the mayor's office.
French intelligence services are monitoring mobilisation by the radical far right after Deranque's death, a security source said, noting he had become a European-level rallying point and expressing concern about reprisal ‚Äåattacks.
Responding to ‚ÅÝa question about external influence on France's presidential election next year, Foreign Minister Jean-Noel Barrot warned about far-right efforts to weaken democracy following the killing.
"Our responsibility is to prevent it from doing so and to do so to regain control of our public space," Barrot said on franceinfo television last week, amid French indignation at statements from the U.S. State Department and Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni warning of the threat posed by the left.
Some European governments have barred entry to far-right figures in recent years. Switzerland and Germany have denied access to Austria's Sellner, and Britain declined visa-free entry to Dutch influencer Eva Vlaardingerbroek.
"These measures just create more attention and more interest in what I ‚Äãhave to say," Sellner told Reuters. Vlaardingerbroek did not comment.
A ‚ÄãGreek official said they were monitoring the far-right ‚ÅÝreaction to Deranque's death. Switzerland's intelligence service said it monitors violent right-wing extremists known to maintain international contacts. German, Italian and British authorities did not comment on security concerns around far-right groups and their cross-border reach.
The Lyon march brought together diverse strands of the far-right, including groups with ties to prominent far-right ‚Äãparties such as France's RN and Germany's AfD.
"As alternative filmmakers, it is our foremost duty to spread the news of the murder of the young patriot as ‚Äãwidely as possible," German media ‚ÅÝcollective Filmkunstkollektiv wrote on its Telegram channel about attending the Lyon march.
Simon Kaupert, the group's founder, told Reuters it had worked with the AfD and Identitarian movement branches in Austria, Germany and France.
More than 30 members of Italian group Lealta Azione - whose activists have campaigned with and stood for election alongside Meloni's party and Deputy Prime Minister Matteo Salvini's far-right League party ‚Äì travelled to Lyon for the march.
The official organiser and point of contact for French authorities was Aliette Espieux, an ‚ÅÝanti-abortion activist who ‚Äãran in the 2020 municipal elections on a joint RN‚ÄìChristian Democratic Party list titled "For the love of Lyon".
The AfD, Lealta Azione, RN and ‚ÄãEspieux did not comment.
"The right only has potential if it works together," said a German far-right activist and influencer known online as Arminius, who attended the march and declined to give his real name.
"Networking with other European activists is extremely important to learn from each other ‚Äãand work together on greater projects," he said.

item.title: Trump hosts Germany's Merz against backdrop of Iran strikes, tariff threats
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump hosts German Chancellor Friedrich Merz at the White House on ‚ÄãTuesday for talks on sensitive topics ranging from U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran to Trump's new tariff threats and the German leader's ‚Äårecent visit to China .
Merz left Berlin for Washington as Germany and France announced plans to deepen cooperation on nuclear deterrence , another move by the European neighbors to adapt to changes in the transatlantic relationship amid ongoing threats from Russia and feared instability linked to the Iran conflict.
Merz, fresh off the heels of a visit with Chinese President Xi ‚ÄãJinping, will work to maintain the positive relationship he has forged with Trump over the past year, aided by Germany's leadership ‚Äãin raising its defense spending.
But that will require delicate diplomacy given European concerns over the legality of the Iran ‚ÅÝstrikes under international law, and deep worries over Trump's threat to pile fresh tariffs on global goods .
He will be the first European leader ‚Äãto visit Washington in the aftermath of the Iran attacks - which have blocked one of the world's key oil shipping lanes and thrown global air transport ‚Äãinto chaos - and the Supreme Court's ruling on February 20 that Trump's emergency tariffs are illegal.
Initially expected to focus on trade, the discussions will likely be dominated by the U.S.-Israeli attack that killed Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and other Iranian leaders over the weekend.
On Sunday, Merz expressed no criticism of the U.S. airstrikes but stopped ‚Äãshort of endorsing an operation which Trump's critics have said was undertaken without sufficient explanation and the required legal backing in international law.
"We recognize ‚Äãthe dilemma," he said, explaining that repeated attempts over past decades had not put Iran off trying to acquire nuclear weapons or oppressing its own people. "So ‚Äåwe're ‚ÅÝnot going to be lecturing our partners on their military strikes against Iran."
Jeff Rathke, president of the American-German Institute, a Washington-based think tank, said the Trump administration did not expect much from the meeting, with no major investment announcements on the horizon.
"It makes it inevitable that the U.S. and Israeli attacks in Iran will be more of a focal point," which could prove risky for Merz, Rathke said. "He might be asked ‚Äãdirectly whether Germany supports the U.S. ‚Äãand whether Germany would provide ‚ÅÝmaterial support to the U.S. campaign, if asked."
Charles Lichfield, director of economic analysis at the Atlantic Council's GeoEconomics Center, said Trump was likely keen to speak with Merz about his visit to China and gather details ‚Äãahead of his own visit there in less than a month.
"Merz can tell Trump about what ‚Äãhe heard and what ‚ÅÝhe saw in China, and say, 'We need to do something together. We'll be stronger against China together,'" he said, noting that industrial overcapacity and global imbalances were key elements of the U.S. agenda for the Group of 20 nations this year.
Merz might also use the trip to press Trump ‚ÅÝfor more ‚Äãdetail on what he plans to do next on Iran, said Julianne Smith, who ‚Äãserved as U.S. ambassador to NATO under former President Joe Biden.
"So, if nothing else, it can be a fact-finding mission to try to determine, 'Do you guys have a plan for the ‚Äãday after?'" she said.

item.title: US orders non-emergency staff to leave Bahrain, Iraq and Jordan
item.description:
DUBAI, March 3 (Reuters) - The U.S. ‚ÄåState Department said on Tuesday ‚Äãit ‚Äãhas ordered the ‚ÅÝdeparture of ‚Äãnon-emergency U.S. ‚Äãgovernment personnel and family members from ‚ÄãBahrain, ‚ÄãIraq and Jordan.
The order ‚Äåcomes ‚ÅÝas the U.S. monitors security risk ‚Äãlinked ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝits conflict ‚Äãwith Iran.

item.title: Malaysia PM tells parliament of plot to destabilise government
item.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 3 (Reuters) - Malaysia's prime minister said a suspect in an alleged plot to topple the government had engaged an international public relations firm to launch a coordinated attack ‚Äãaimed at undermining national institutions before the next election.
Police said last week they ‚Äåwere investigating an alleged conspiracy to "sabotage national stability", under laws against undermining parliamentary democracy.
In an address to parliament on Tuesday, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim provided further details of the alleged plot, saying the suspect ‚Äãhad engaged the PR firm as part of a response to being the ‚Äãsubject of a large-scale graft investigation by the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC).
Neither Anwar, ‚ÅÝnor the police, have identified the individual suspect or the PR firm.
Anwar said the ‚ÄãPR strategy, which began in August 2024 and included engaging media agencies, banks, and lawmakers, ‚Äãwas to run until the next general election, due by early 2028.
"Their strategy... was to contact all foreign media with a strategy of undermining the government's efforts, especially the MACC's," Anwar told parliament, citing ‚Äãdocuments obtained by authorities.
"And then to use their power and contacts in foreign countries to ‚Äãshape a narrative questioning the authority of the Malaysian government and organise a movement through the Malaysian ‚Äåparliament. ‚ÅÝThat is what worries us."
Anwar said documents identified media firm Bloomberg as one of the agencies targeted by the alleged conspiracy.
A Bloomberg spokesperson did not immediately respond to an emailed request for comment on Anwar's remarks.
A special government committee is conducting a separate investigation into allegations ‚Äãin a Bloomberg report ‚Äãlast month that ‚ÅÝhead of MACC, Azam Baki, may have breached shareholding laws.
Azam has said he is willing to be investigated as he had "nothing to ‚Äãhide" and all his financial declarations have been made according to ‚Äãpublic service laws.
Lawmakers, ‚ÅÝincluding a key party in Anwar's ruling coalition, have called for a royal inquiry into another Bloomberg article alleging broader misconduct at the anti-graft agency.
The MACC has said the allegations were "baseless", and that ‚ÅÝthey ‚Äãwere an attempt to discredit its investigations and enforcement ‚Äãactions within the corporate sector.
On Tuesday, Anwar said a royal inquiry into the matter was "premature" as the special committee ‚Äãwas expected to complete its probe this week.

item.title: Europe's skimpy gas storage under scrutiny as Qatar halts LNG flows
item.description:
LITTLETON, Colorado, March 3 (Reuters) - The historically low levels of gas inventories across Europe could quickly become a major liability for utilities and industry across the region following the halting of LNG exports by world's third-largest producer Qatar.
A ‚Äãcombination of new gas storage rules, high natural gas prices, above-normal winter temperatures and subdued regional economic activity prompted Europe's gas ‚Äåstorage operators to deplete stockpiles to well below normal this winter.
The prospect of record exports of liquefied natural gas from the likes of the United States and Qatar had also lifted expectations that international gas markets would be brimming with gas supplies throughout 2026.
However, following drone strikes by Iran on Qatar's main LNG export facility this weekend, the prevailing narrative ‚Äãof persistent LNG abundance has now given way to expectations of shipment delays and sharply higher prices.
Benchmark wholesale natural gas prices ‚Äãin Europe jumped by over 30% on Monday compared to the end of last week as market participants scrambled ‚ÅÝto price in the impact of the loss of supplies from Qatar.
While Europe secured only 7% of LNG supplies from Qatar in 2025, according to ‚ÄãKpler, the knock-on effects of any protracted Qatar LNG shutdown will likely impact all LNG trade flows, especially to the world's top buyers in Asia.
China - the ‚Äãworld's top LNG importer in 2025 - relied on Qatar for 29% of LNG imports last year, while number-four LNG buyer India relied on Qatar for around 45% of supplies.
To plug any potential shortfalls stemming from the Qatar shutdowns, China, India, Japan and South Korea will all likely now step up order interest from other vendors, leading to tighter overall ‚ÄãLNG supplies to all destinations.
While the timeline of Qatar's outages remains unclear, major gas consumers and storage operators across Europe must quickly decide ‚Äãhow to fend off any risks of further gas depletion while avoiding getting caught up in any panic-buying that could lift global LNG prices even higher.
The problem is, with ‚Äågas inventories ‚ÅÝalready at multi-year or record lows, many of Europe's top gas consumers may have no choice but to step up buying even if gas costs keep climbing.
Natural gas inventories in Germany - Europe's largest gas consumer - started the month of March at only 27% of capacity, compared to an average of 64% of capacity for that time of year since 2023, data from LSEG shows.
Gas stockpiles in the Netherlands - home to Europe's main gas trading hub - are only ‚Äãaround 10% of capacity, compared to ‚Äãaround 48% on average for early ‚ÅÝMarch.
Italy - which is Europe's second-largest gas consumer - currently has gas inventories at around 50% of capacity, which is a far better cushion than most northern peers but is still less than the average of 60% full in early ‚ÄãMarch.
Overall, Europe's gas stockpiles across major markets are currently around 30% full compared to nearly 54% full for the ‚Äãearly March period.
That ‚ÅÝmeans that nearly all major gas consumers in the region are under pressure to rebuild those stocks going forward, even in the face of heightened global competition for available supplies and already-rising prices.
The opinions expressed here are those of the author , a columnist for Reuters.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), your essential new source ‚ÅÝfor global ‚Äãfinancial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . ‚ÄãSubscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance 7 days a week.

item.title: France says it will work with China on de-esclatating Iran war
item.description:
PARIS, March 3 (Reuters) - French Foreign Minister Jean-Noel Barrot held a call on Monday with his ‚ÄãChinese counterpart Wang Yi to discuss ‚Äåthe Iran war and both agreed to work on de-escalation, Barrot's office said in a statement.
Both ministers ‚Äãcommitted to seeking a political solution ‚Äãthat would guarantee collective security and take ‚ÅÝinto account the aspirations of the ‚ÄãIranian people, the statement said.
Barrot "reiterated the Iranian ‚Äãregime's responsibility for the ongoing escalation, after it unjustifiably attacked several countries in the region," the statement ‚Äãsaid.
He also criticised the Iranian regime for ‚Äãrefusing to respect the resolutions from the United Nations' ‚ÄåSecurity ‚ÅÝCouncil on the nuclear programmes, ballistic activities, support to non-state armed groups and rejecting good-faith multilateral negotiations.
The French minister reiterated that ‚ÄãFrance was ‚Äãnot involved ‚ÅÝin the actions by the United States and Israel and ‚Äãhad no prior knowledge of them. ‚ÄãCountries "must ‚ÅÝgive precedence to international institutions to solve disputes and, where needed, the use of force," ‚ÅÝhe ‚Äãadded.
The two ministers agreed ‚Äãto keep dialogue open on the issue.

item.title: Dollar gets its mojo back - but only by default
item.description:
LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - While it's tempting to assume the dollar's long-lost "safety" bid has returned since the weekend Iran attacks , it's not as clear-cut as it seems and owes more to relative energy plays. Yet the implications of the market response may be just as powerful.
Ever since Donald Trump's return to the White House last year, the dollar has waned even during periods of market anxiety and volatility, due in ‚Äãlarge part to U.S. economic policy uncertainty and both domestic and geopolitical upheaval.
Reversing years of dollar over-valuation is a key tenet of the Trump administration's economic plan. But the greenback's diminished haven role in times of ‚Äåglobal political or financial stress suggests foreign investors - already up to their eyeballs in U.S. assets - have changed their behaviour.
So it was remarkable that the dollar (.DXY) , opens new tab jumped across the board after last weekend's extraordinary bombing campaign by U.S. and Israeli forces against Iranian targets, including the assassination of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and the wave of regional violence that's followed.
The crux of the move hinged more on the inevitable energy price dynamics rather than any dash for dollars per se. In fact, it was more a default move out of the currencies of economies ‚Äãworst hit by an outsized and protracted energy price squeeze.
With the U.S. now a net exporter of total petroleum and energy products in general, the initial 10% surge in world oil prices on Monday hurt other ‚Äãmajor currencies much more due to fears of a major demand hit if the supply hiatus persists for several weeks or even months.
That's why other traditional havens ‚ÅÝsuch as Japan's yen , caught no safety bid this time around and plunged over 1% against the dollar on Monday given Japan's big energy import bill and the fact that about a third of its energy imports comes through the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz.
China too is a big consumer of oil now stuck in those contentious waterways, particularly deeply discounted Iranian crude that's sanctioned in the West and now also in limbo. The recently high-flying yuan turned tail on Monday and dropped 0.8% as the ‚Äãsituation unfolded.
"This isn‚Äôt a friendly outcome for the Northern Asian currencies," said Societe Generale currency strategist Kit Juckes, adding that the most important indication from Trump so far has been that the U.S. action will take weeks , not days.
For Europe, the calculation is compounded by its exposure to natural gas after the shipping attacks effectively closed the Hormuz route, a conduit for 20% of worldwide liquefied natural gas shipments and up to 30% of crude oil.
Benchmark European gas prices surged by almost 50% at one point on Monday to their highest in more than ‚Äãa year, closing up 35% and prompting the European Union's gas supply group to schedule an emergency meeting for Wednesday.
The U.S. supplied 58% of the European Union's LNG last year. Qatar, which accounted for 6% of the bloc's imports, shut down its ‚Äãproduction plants on Monday after attacks from Iran.
The euro fell 1% against the dollar to its lowest in more than a month.
The Swiss franc's long-standing and often unwelcome haven status remains in play - but it's complicated by the Swiss National Bank's battle against deflation ‚Äåand its restated ‚ÅÝcommitment to intervene to sell francs to cap the unit.
As to the overall economic hit from an oil spike worldwide, Barclays economists assume every sustained $10 per barrel rise in crude prices takes up to 0.2 percentage point off global growth. And if a wave of forecasts of $100-plus per barrel were to prove accurate, then that could well bite.
As it stands, however, Monday's net Brent crude price rise of $5 to $77 per barrel will be a much more modest blow - and the moves so far would barely have any significant demand impacts on the U.S. itself.
Calculations then turn to whether oil price pressure becomes an economic depressant or inflation aggravator. With U.S. core inflation running above 3%, ‚Äãthat could argue for more focus on the latter and ‚Äãfor keeping U.S. interest rates high through the year - ‚ÅÝanother support for the dollar.
But, as so often with Middle East conflicts, the initial ready-reckoners on global economic hits all hinge on duration of conflict and the energy supply disruption.
Trump has indicated the military campaign will run for four or five weeks and, likely riffing off that, prediction markets such as Polymarket see a 63% chance Trump will ‚Äãcall a halt by the end of this month.
And yet most of the thinking on currency reactions is not strictly calculations of dollar hoarding or cross-border dash ‚Äãfor safety - rather they seem ‚ÅÝjust like relative economic assessments emanating from energy exposure.
But for all that, it can have a powerful and looping effect.
Barclays' rule of thumb for the dollar, for example, is that it gains between 0.5% and 1.0% for every $10 increase in oil.
If dollar‚Äëdenominated energy prices rise and stay high, pushing the exchange rate up with them, that would both worsen the energy shock for overseas economies and drive the dollar even higher in a self‚Äëreinforcing loop.
No one would want that scenario - least of ‚ÅÝall Washington.
(The opinions ‚Äãexpressed here are those of the author , a columnist for Reuters.}
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global ‚Äãfinancial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
Plus, sign up for my weekday newsletter, Morning Bid U.S. and listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Israel prepared for weeks-long Iran campaign, unlikely to deploy ground forces, spokesperson says
item.description:
Tel Aviv, March 3 (Reuters) - The Israeli military has ‚Äãprepared for a campaign against Iran ‚Äåthat could last several weeks but is unlikely to see the deployment of ground ‚Äãforces, a military spokesperson told reporters ‚Äãon Tuesday.
"We have prepared a general ‚ÅÝscope of weeks," Lieutenant Colonel Nadav ‚ÄãShoshani said in an online briefing, adding ‚Äãthat the duration of the military campaign could change, depending on developments. He described progress ‚Äãso far as positive.
When asked if ‚ÄãIsrael could deploy ground forces to Iran, Shoshani said ‚Äåthat ‚ÅÝwas unlikely.
"I don't think that's something very likely at the moment for Israeli forces. There's not a practical idea ‚Äãat the ‚Äãmoment that ‚ÅÝI know of," he said.
Israel and the U.S. have been ‚Äãcarrying out air strikes against ‚ÄãIran ‚ÅÝsince Saturday, triggering Iranian retaliation .
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said on Monday that the U.S.-Israel ‚ÅÝwar ‚Äãwith Iran could take "some ‚Äãtime" but would not take years.

item.title: Progress on rules for lethal autonomous weapons urgently needed, says chair of Geneva talks
item.description:
GENEVA, March 3 (Reuters) - Progress on a potential international framework to prohibit and restrict Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems (LAWS) is urgently needed, as talks on the matter in Geneva enter a ‚Äãcrucial phase, their chair said.
From this week to the mandate's end in September, ‚Äå128 states will discuss whether to agree by consensus a non-binding text that could pave the way for future negotiations on prohibitions and regulations on LAWS.
Since 2014, more than a hundred states party to the Convention on ‚ÄãCertain Conventional Weapons have met in the Swiss city to discuss banning LAWS that ‚Äãdo not comply with existing international law while regulating others.
"If we wait then ‚ÅÝit almost gets to a stage where you're too late... We will be overtaken by ‚Äãtechnological developments," Robert in den Bosch, the Dutch Disarmament Ambassador in Geneva and Chair of the CCW ‚ÄãGroup of Governmental Experts on LAWS, told Reuters.
There are mounting concerns over the role of AI-assisted semi-autonomous weapons that have been used in conflicts in Ukraine, Sudan, Gaza, and in Iran and the Gulf , opens new tab .
While states agree international humanitarian law (IHL) ‚Äãapplies to LAWS, specific internationally binding standards for these systems remain virtually non-existent.
Russia and the ‚ÄãUnited States, among others, oppose new legally binding instruments, arguing existing laws suffice.
In den Bosch stated that others say ‚Äånew ‚ÅÝrules are needed to bridge supposed accountability gaps in IHL, which place obligations on states and individuals, not on machines.
The Rolling Text under discussion in Geneva proposes "context appropriate human judgment and control" as a measure to ensure that systems that "identify, select and engage" targets without human intervention comply ‚Äãwith IHL.
Despite growing calls for ‚Äãurgent regulation, U.N. ‚ÅÝSecretary-General Antonio Guterres' deadline for agreeing a legally binding instrument on LAWS this year will realistically be missed, In den Bosch said.
While the deadline is ‚Äãoutside the Geneva talks' remit, the challenge of securing consensus on even ‚Äãnon-binding elements ‚ÅÝunderscores the difficulty of making progress, In den Bosch stated.
The talks are set within a challenging context of geopolitical tensions and recent European withdrawals from the landmine ban treaty over Russian threats.
The Review Conference of ‚ÅÝthe CCW ‚Äãin November could decide to launch negotiations for a ‚Äãbinding protocol after the Geneva talks end. However, in the absence of agreement, there is a risk some countries might ‚Äãpursue a breakaway treaty elsewhere, In den Bosch stated.

item.title: Israeli military deploys additional forces to southern Lebanon
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 3 (Reuters) - The Israeli military deployed ‚Äåadditional forces to southern Lebanon overnight, to take up what a military ‚Äãspokesperson on Tuesday described as ‚Äãdefensive positions to protect Israeli ‚ÅÝcivilians and strategic sites from ‚Äãany potential Hezbollah attack.
"We're only at ‚Äãthe borderline area in a defensive manner to prevent attacks against civilians and ‚Äãvery strategic important points," Lieutenant ‚ÄãColonel Nadav Shoshani said in an online ‚Äåbriefing ‚ÅÝwith reporters.
Defence Minister Israel Katz said in a statement he had authorised the military to advance ‚Äãand take ‚Äãcontrol ‚ÅÝof additional positions in Lebanon after Hezbollah fired rockets ‚Äãat Israel late on Sunday, ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝreaction to U.S. and Israeli strikes against Iran .
Israeli forces have occupied ‚ÅÝfive ‚Äãpositions in southern Lebanon ‚Äãsince November 2024.

item.title: France ready to defend partners, Foreign Minister says
item.description:
PARIS, March 3 (Reuters) - France is ready to defend its partners if ‚Äãthey request help after Iranian ‚Äåretaliatory strikes and ireserves its right to intervene, Foreign Minister Jean-Noel ‚ÄãBarrot said on Tuesday.
"This ‚Äãwar... is dragging a number ‚ÅÝof countries in the region ‚Äãinto the conflict with which ‚Äãwe have relations, defence agreements, and interests, including military bases," Barrot told ‚ÄãFrench broadcaster BFM TV.
He added ‚Äãthat French Rafale fighter jets carried out ‚Äåair ‚ÅÝoperations to secure the skies above France's bases in the region. Around 400,000 French ‚Äãnationals are ‚Äãcurrently ‚ÅÝin the countries affected by the crisis in ‚Äãthe Middle East and ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝFrench government is ready to fly back those most at ‚ÅÝrisk ‚Äãusing both commercial ‚Äãand military flights, Barrot said.

item.title: Drone hits fuel tank at Oman's Duqm port
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - A fuel tank at Oman‚Äôs Duqm commercial port ‚Äãwas hit on Tuesday when the facility came under ‚Äåattack from several unmanned aircraft, the state news agency said, citing a security source, on the fourth day of U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran , which ‚Äãhas responded by targeting neighbouring countries.
The attack comes a ‚Äãday after Qatar halted its production of liquefied ‚ÅÝnatural gas - about a fifth of global supply - while Saudi Arabia suspended ‚Äãproduction at its largest domestic refinery.
Major Israeli gas fields, including Leviathan, ‚Äãwere offline, and most output in the Iraqi Kurdish region had been shut down as Iran continued to target infrastructure, including energy facilities, ports ‚Äãand airports, over recent days.
Oman had been acting as ‚Äãmediator in talks between the U.S. and Iran over Tehran's nuclear programme before ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝattacks on Iran.
The resulting damage at Duqm was contained and no casualties were recorded, Oman's news agency added. Duqm had also been targeted by two drones on Sunday, wounding one ‚Äãworker. Oman had ‚Äãbeen spared from ‚ÅÝattacks on Saturday, the day the U.S. and Israel launched their attack.
The Abu Dhabi government's ‚Äãmedia office said on Monday a fire broke ‚Äãout ‚ÅÝafter the Musaffah fuel tank terminal was targeted by a drone, without impacting its operations.
Abu Dhabi state oil giant ADNOC operates a ‚ÅÝfacility ‚Äãin Musaffah from which fuels are ‚Äãtransported by trucks and through a 1,600-kilometre (994 miles) pipeline network.

item.title: Trump says 'sad to see' US-UK relationship is not what it was
item.description:
LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said it was "sad to see" the relationship with Britain was "not what it was" after ‚ÄãPrime Minister Keir Starmer initially held off giving military support to the strikes ‚Äåagainst Iran.
Trump said the likes of France had been more supportive and said he had never expected to see the once "most solid of all" relationships change in ‚Äãthis way.
"It's very sad to see that the relationship is obviously ‚Äãnot what it was," Trump told the Sun newspaper on Tuesday, ‚ÅÝhis second interview to a British newspaper in as many days where ‚Äãhe criticised the British leader.
Starmer said late on Sunday that he would ‚Äãallow the U.S. to use British military bases for defensive strikes after they were not used in the initial attack on Iran.
Trump said the U.S. did not need Britain to ‚Äãwage war in the Middle East but added: "It's not going to matter, ‚Äãbut (Starmer) should have helped‚Ä¶ he should have.
"I mean, France has been great. They've all ‚Äåbeen ‚ÅÝgreat. The UK has been much different from others."
Senior British minister Darren Jones told Times Radio in response to the latest Trump comment's that the U.S.-UK relationship remained crucial but said the country had learnt lessons ‚Äãfrom its involvement ‚Äãin the 2003 ‚ÅÝIraq war.
"One of the lessons of Iraq was that it's better to be involved in these situations when ‚Äãyou are aligned with international partners, and as I ‚Äãsay, with ‚ÅÝa clear legal basis in the plan," he said.
Starmer said in parliament on Monday: "President Trump has expressed his disagreement with our decision not to get ‚ÅÝinvolved ‚Äãin the initial strikes, but it is ‚Äãmy duty to judge what is in Britain‚Äôs national interest. That is what I have done, ‚Äãand I stand by it."

item.title: France to send¬Ýanti-drone systems to Cyprus after British base attack, report says
item.description:
NICOSIA, March 3 (Reuters) - France plans to send anti-missile and anti-drone systems to Cyprus after a British air base ‚Äãon the island was attacked by drones, the semi-official Cyprus News ‚ÄåAgency (CNA) said on Tuesday.
France would send anti-missile and anti-drone systems, as well as a frigate, CNA reported. It said French President Emmanuel Macron communicated the intention to Cypriot President Nikos ‚ÄãChristodoulides early on Tuesday.
A Cypriot government source, speaking on condition of anonymity, ‚Äãconfirmed the report. The French defence ministry did not immediately respond ‚ÅÝto a request for comment.
The reported French aid to the island follows ‚ÄãGreece's offer of assistance hours after RAF Akrotiri, a British sovereign base, was targeted ‚Äãin two separate drone incidents on Monday.
In the first instance an Iranian-made Shahed drone crashed into the runway of the base, causing limited damage. Hours later two other drones were intercepted.
Senior Cypriot ‚Äãofficials say the attack was carried out by an Iranian Shahed drone most ‚Äãlikely fired by Iran-backed militant group Hezbollah from Lebanon. The officials have stressed that the ‚Äåbase - ‚ÅÝconsidered sovereign British territory - rather than Cyprus, was the target.
Britain said on Sunday that it had accepted a U.S. request to use its military bases for "defensive" strikes against Iran, a day after the launch of a U.S. and Israeli military campaign against ‚ÄãIran , which triggered ‚ÄãIranian retaliation. British ‚ÅÝPrime Minister Keir Starmer later said Britain's bases on Cyprus were not being used by U.S. bombers.
Cyprus has asked Britain that ‚Äãthe bases only be used for humanitarian purposes.
Greece on Monday ‚Äãdispatched four ‚ÅÝF-16 fighter jets to the island. Two frigates, one equipped with its anti-drone Centauros jamming system, were also sailing to Cyprus.
Centauros, which has been successfully deployed against Houthi ‚ÅÝrebels ‚Äãattacking shipping lanes off Yemen, has the capability ‚Äãof detecting and incapacitating low-flying drones, such as the one which crashed into RAF Akrotiri and evaded ‚Äãradars, Cypriot defence sources said.

item.title: Russia says it has seen no evidence that Iran was developing nuclear weapons
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 3 (Reuters) - Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov said on Tuesday that Moscow had still seen no evidence that Iran ‚Äãwas developing nuclear weapons as the U.S. and Israel ‚Äåpursue their attack.
U.S. President Donald Trump has offered various reasons for going to war with Iran. On Monday, in his most extensive public ‚Äãcomments so far, he said he had ordered ‚Äãthe attack to thwart Tehran's nuclear and ballistic ‚ÅÝmissile programmes.
Lavrov told Brunei's foreign minister during talks ‚Äãin Moscow: "We still see no evidence that Iran was ‚Äãdeveloping nuclear weapons, which was the main, if not the only, justification for the war."
He said the consequences of the attack ‚Äãon Iran were being felt throughout the region, and ‚Äãthat Arab countries were bearing economic costs and suffering casualties.
He reiterated ‚ÄåRussia's ‚ÅÝcall for an immediate cessation of hostilities by all parties:
"As an unconditional first step, we must do everything possible to stop any actions that result in ‚Äãcivilian casualties."
He pointed ‚Äãto the ‚ÅÝreported bombing of a school in Iran.
Iranian state media have accused Israel and the ‚ÄãU.S. of a strike on a girls' ‚Äãprimary ‚ÅÝschool in the southern Iranian town that Tehran said had killed more than 160 people.
U.S. Secretary of State ‚ÅÝMarco ‚ÄãRubio said U.S. forces would not ‚Äãdeliberately attack a school.

item.title: Israeli military says striking Hezbollah command centers, arms depots in Beirut
item.description: March 3 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄãIsraeli military ‚Äåsaid early ‚Äãon ‚ÄãTuesday that ‚ÅÝit ‚Äãwas ‚Äãcarrying out strikes ‚Äãon ‚ÄãHezbollah command centers ‚Äåand ‚ÅÝweapons storage ‚Äãfacilities ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝBeirut.

item.title: IAEA confirms entrances to Iran's Natanz enrichment plant were bombed
item.description:
VIENNA, March 3 (Reuters) - Entrances to Iran's underground and ‚Äãpreviously bombed uranium-enrichment plant at Natanz ‚Äåhave been struck as part of the U.S.-Israeli military attacks on the country, the U.N. ‚Äãnuclear watchdog confirmed on Tuesday.
The underground ‚ÄãFuel Enrichment Plant (FEP) is one of Iran's ‚ÅÝthree uranium-enrichment plants that are known ‚Äãto have been operating when Israel and ‚Äãthe United States carried out strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities last June.
"Based on the latest available satellite ‚Äãimagery, IAEA can now confirm some ‚Äãrecent damage to entrance buildings of Iran's underground ‚ÄåNatanz ‚ÅÝFuel Enrichment Plant," the International Atomic Energy Agency said on X.
"No radiological consequence expected and no additional impact detected at FEP ‚Äãitself, which ‚Äãwas ‚ÅÝseverely damaged in the June conflict," it added.
The IAEA's finding fits ‚Äãwith that of U.S. think-tank ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝInstitute for Science and International Security published on Monday after Iran said Natanz was hit ‚ÅÝon ‚ÄãSunday and the IAEA responded ‚Äãthat any military strikes were not major.

item.title: At least 30,000 displaced people in shelters in Lebanon, says UN refugee agency
item.description:
GENEVA, March 3 (Reuters) - At least 30,000 displaced ‚Äãpeople have sought protection ‚Äåin shelters in Lebanon since hostilities ‚Äãbetween Israel and ‚ÄãHezbollah began on Monday, the United ‚ÅÝNations refugee agency ‚Äãsaid on Tuesday.
"Conservative ‚Äãestimates suggest that nearly 30,000 people were hosted ‚Äãand registered at ‚Äãcollective shelters," said UNHCR spokesperson ‚ÄåBabar ‚ÅÝBaloch.
"Many more slept in their cars on the side ‚Äãof ‚Äãroads ‚ÅÝor were still stuck in ‚Äãtraffic jams ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝthe roads," he added.

item.title: Key Gaza crossing will be reopened, says World Food Programme
item.description:
GENEVA, March 3 (Reuters) - A key aid crossing ‚Äåthat had been momentarily closed into Gaza will reopen imminently, the United Nations World Food Programme said ‚Äãon Tuesday.
Humanitarian supplies will be able ‚Äãto enter through the Kerem Shalom ‚ÅÝcrossing into the enclave between Tuesday and ‚ÄãWednesday, the WFP said.
"That is timely for ‚Äãus, and we need to get in aid as fast as we can," Samer Abdel Jaber, ‚ÄãWFP Regional Director for the Middle ‚ÄãEast, North Africa, and Eastern Europe, told reporters in Geneva ‚Äåvia ‚ÅÝvideo link from Cairo.
Israeli government agency COGAT said it will reopen the Kerem Shalom crossing starting on Tuesday for the gradual ‚Äãentry of humanitarian ‚Äãaid ‚ÅÝinto the Gaza Strip.
COGAT said on Saturday that crossings into the ‚ÄãGaza Strip, vital for the ‚Äãdelivery ‚ÅÝof humanitarian aid and the movement of patients in need of medical evacuation, were ‚ÅÝclosed ‚Äãas Israeli and U.S. ‚Äãforces attacked Iran.

item.title: World Cup ticket frenzy unfolds amid global unrest
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - With 100 days to go until the tournament kicks off, appetite for tickets to the ‚Äã2026 World Cup in the U.S., Mexico and Canada is reaching fever pitch, despite eye-watering prices that have fans crying foul amid global unrest ‚Äåfollowing the U.S. attack on Iran.
In addition to the U.S.-Israeli strikes against Iran -- a country scheduled to play its World Cup group stage games in the U.S. -- the heavy-handed crackdowns by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement personnel and the violence that erupted near host city Guadalajara after the death of Mexico's most-wanted cartel leader are causing concern for fans.
"I'm afraid I might not be allowed into the ‚Äãcountry, I've decided to fly to Canada at most, but not to the USA," German soccer fan Tom Roeder told Reuters.
"I hope that at least the ‚Äãissue of war with Iran does not reach North America. At least not in a way that affects us personally."
FIFA, which ‚ÅÝdid not immediately respond to a request for comment, has said nearly two million tickets were sold in the first two sales phases, with demand so intense that World ‚ÄãCup tickets were oversubscribed over 30 times.
Political and social tensions surrounding host nations are nothing new for the World Cup.
Mexico President Claudia Sheinbaum said there was " no risk " ‚Äãto fans coming to the country and Adrian Nunez Corte, leader of Unipes, a fans' association in Spain, said it has not affected willingness to buy tickets.
"Obviously, it is causing concern, but some Spanish fans living in the area have helped to calm things down after the initial hours of alarm," Corte said.
"There is no alarm regarding U.S. immigration policy but people are taking preparation of ‚Äãthe necessary visas seriously to avoid problems, especially since some fans will be travelling between the U.S. and Mexico due to the match schedule."
The ‚Äãbuzz around the tournament in North America is unprecedented.
"The demand for the 2026 World Cup in the USA, Canada and Mexico is the strongest I've ever experienced," said Michael Edgley, director at ‚ÄåAustralia's Green ‚ÅÝand Gold Army Travel.
"I think FIFA will make record amounts of money. There's no question.
"This World Cup will be a massive financial success and the beneficiaries will be the member federations."
But such popularity comes with a price.
Geography adds another layer of complexity as the tournament spans 16 host cities across three countries, making it more challenging and expensive for fans wanting to follow their teams.
"The price of tickets has been a major drawback, particularly affecting the number of matches each fan will attend, as well as the ‚Äãdistances between venues and the costs involved," ‚Äãsaid Corte.
The sticker shock ‚ÅÝis even more pronounced this year, especially with a huge resale market where tickets are sold at above face value, which is legal in the United States and Canada.
FIFA defended the secondary market model.
"Unlike the entities behind profit-driven third-party ticket marketplaces, FIFA is ‚Äãa not-for-profit organisation," a spokesperson said.
"Revenue generated from the FIFA World Cup 2026 ticket sales model is reinvested into the global ‚Äãdevelopment of football... FIFA ‚ÅÝexpects to reinvest more than 90% of its budgeted investment for the 2023-2026 cycle back into the game."
Mehdi Salem, vice-president of French football fans association Les Baroudeurs du Sport, said they are seeing more than a 200% increase on what they were told in 2018 by the French federation and by FIFA.
The pricing pain is so acute that Salem's association, which ‚ÅÝboasts around ‚Äã400 members, will have only 100 attend the tournament - a dramatic drop that he attributes to ticket ‚Äãprices and the political landscape in the United States.
"We feel like this World Cup will not really be a people's World Cup, but rather an elitist World Cup," Salem added.
(This story has been refiled to add the first name, designation, and the tour operator, in paragraph 11)

item.title: Turkey eyes lifting U.S. CAATSA sanctions before U.S. midterms
item.description:
ANKARA, March 3 (Reuters) - Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said work was underway with the United States to lift sanctions imposed ‚Äãon Ankara before the U.S. midterm elections in November, ‚Äåbut added Israel was opposed to the potential move.
In 2020, Washington imposed CAATSA sanctions on fellow NATO member Turkey over its acquisition of ‚ÄãRussian S-400 systems. It also removed Ankara from ‚Äãthe F-35 fighter jet programme, a move Turkey called ‚ÅÝunjust and illegal.
The sides are seeking a workaround to lift ‚Äãsanctions and allow Ankara to buy the jets. Little progress ‚Äãhas been made even though U.S. President Donald Trump has good ties with Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan, raising hopes in Turkey that a ‚Äãsolution can be found this year.
Asked during a Ramadan fast-breaking ‚Äãdinner on Tuesday with members of Turkish media if Israel - which Turkey ‚Äåhas ‚ÅÝcriticised fiercely over its assault on Gaza - opposed the U.S. lifting the sanctions, Fidan said Israel did not want regional countries to obtain certain capabilities.
"That is the mode they are ‚Äãin now. Let's ‚Äãsee, these ‚ÅÝbattles will continue. Everyone will play the cards in their hands somehow," he said, according ‚Äãto a transcript of his comments on Wednesday.
"Work ‚Äãis ‚ÅÝunderway to obtain a result on CAATSA before the midterms. We have taken the political steps. We'll see," Fidan added.
Fidan's comments ‚ÅÝcome ‚Äãas the United States and Israel are ‚Äãat war with Iran, Turkey's neighbour. Ankara has condemned the U.S. and ‚ÄãIsraeli offensive against Tehran.

item.title: Lebanon sucked deeper into war as Hezbollah, Israel trade blows
item.description:
BEIRUT/JERUSALEM, March 3 (Reuters) - Lebanon was pulled deeper into the war in the Middle East on Tuesday as the Iran-backed Lebanese group Hezbollah launched ‚Äãmissiles at Israel for a second consecutive day and Israel sent troops into the south and carried out waves of air ‚Äåstrikes.
Israel's neighbour to the north and the theatre of numerous conflicts between Israel and Hezbollah, Lebanon had avoided spillover from the U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran until Monday, when Hezbollah opened fire with drones and missiles.
With dozens of people killed in retaliatory airstrikes, Hezbollah's move to enter the conflict has sharpened long-standing divisions in Lebanon over its status as an ‚Äãarmed group - the only Lebanese faction to keep its weapons after the 1975-90 civil war.
The government on Monday took the unprecedented step of ‚Äãoutlawing Hezbollah's military activities. The pro-Hezbollah al-Akhbar newspaper on Tuesday condemned this as a "capitulation to dictates, which could even lead ‚ÅÝto the outbreak of civil war".
Towers of smoke rose from the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs of Beirut as Israel launched new airstrikes.
Many thousands of Lebanese have ‚Äãfled homes in areas that bore the brunt of a major war between Israel and Hezbollah in 2024. The United Nations said that, by Monday, ‚Äãan estimated 29,000 people, including 9,000 children, had fled.
The Israeli military said it had deployed additional forces to southern Lebanon overnight, saying this was to take up defensive positions to guard against any potential Hezbollah attack.
"We're only at the border line area in a defensive manner to prevent attacks against civilians and very strategic important points," Lieutenant Colonel ‚ÄãNadav Shoshani said.
Defence Minister Israel Katz said in a statement he had authorised the military to advance and take control of additional positions.
The Israeli military ‚Äãon Monday night ordered residents of the entire border strip of southern Lebanon to evacuate.
A Lebanese security source said Israeli troops were carrying out incursions along some parts ‚Äåof the ‚ÅÝborder. Witnesses said the Lebanese army had pulled out of at least seven forward-operating positions along the border.
Israel kept some troops in the south at several hilltop positions following a ceasefire in the 2024 war.
The south, predominantly Shi'ite Muslim, has long been a key Hezbollah stronghold, where it has drawn political support and deployed weaponry ahead of the 2024 conflict. The Lebanese army has moved into the area and seized its weapons caches since that conflict, from which ‚ÄãHezbollah emerged greatly weakened.
Hezbollah announced three ‚Äãseparate attacks on Tuesday using attack ‚ÅÝdrones and missiles and saying these had targeted military facilities in northern Israel.
A missile from Lebanon hit a house in northern Israel, Israeli media reported. Israel's ambulance service said a man was treated for ‚Äãglass shrapnel injuries.
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun said rockets fired at Israel on Monday from Lebanon came from outside ‚Äãthe southern border zone ‚ÅÝwhere the army declared its control in January.
The Lebanese health ministry on Monday reported that 52 people had been killed in Israeli attacks in Lebanon and more than 150 wounded. It has not issued an update on Tuesday.
Overnight, an Israeli airstrike hit the headquarters of Hezbollah's al-Manar TV in Beirut.
The Israeli military reported more ‚ÅÝairstrikes in ‚ÄãBeirut on Tuesday, saying it had hit "command centres, weapons storage facilities, and satellite communication components ‚Äãbelonging to Hezbollah‚Äôs intelligence headquarters in Beirut".
"These assets were operating under civilian cover," it said.
The Israeli military said it had taken steps to mitigate civilian harm, including the use of advanced warnings.

item.title: European capitals push back as Ukraine seeks fast-track EU membership
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 3 (Reuters) - Ukraine‚Äôs push for a fast track into the European Union as part of any ‚Äãpeace deal has hit stiff resistance from EU governments, who do not want to open the Pandora‚Äôs box of problems that they fear a quick accession would ‚Äåunleash.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy wants a 2027 date to join the EU as part of a settlement to end Russia's war , hoping this would anchor his country in Europe‚Äôs main political club and offer a path to greater prosperity, security and stability.
A clear route into the EU could be vital for Zelenskiy in selling any peace settlement to Ukrainians, particularly if - as is widely expected - Ukraine does not regain control of all of its territory or join ‚Äãthe NATO military alliance, analysts say.
But EU governments - including heavyweights France and Germany - have privately voiced scepticism about a mooted reform of the accession process that would shorten Ukraine's path ‚Äãto membership, diplomats say. Reuters spoke with eight European diplomats and officials, and many pointed to unease among EU capitals about the idea.
Among their ‚ÅÝconcerns is that Ukraine and others would not press on with reforms , such as cracking down on corruption, if it has already been granted EU membership.
Ukrainian Deputy Prime Minister and lead EU negotiator ‚ÄãTaras Kachka told Reuters that Kyiv was ready to address EU members' concerns . He proposed safeguards such as a monitoring system to check Kyiv was sticking to democratic standards and a transition period before it ‚Äãreceived EU farm subsidies.
But he said a political commitment to a membership date would be important. "This is necessary for the peace process, for the establishing of long-lasting and just peace in Europe," he said.
The current process for joining the bloc is usually long and bureaucratic, even in straightforward cases, involving years of detailed negotiations and legal reforms to meet EU democratic and economic standards.
Every step of the accession process, ‚Äãwhich is divided into chapters and clusters of policy issues, also requires approval from all EU members. Hungary has blocked Ukraine's path early in the process.
European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, head ‚Äãof the bloc‚Äôs executive, has floated a revamp of the process behind closed doors, diplomats say.
Instead of carrying out all the required reforms first, a country could join the EU after meeting some minimum requirements ‚Äì but with ‚Äålimited access to ‚ÅÝEU funds and decision-making until they meet all the membership benchmarks.
That could make it easier for Ukraine and others such as Moldova, Montenegro and Albania to join quickly ‚Äì although Zelenskiy‚Äôs target of January 1, 2027, would still be unrealistic, not least as all 27 current EU members have to ratify any accession.
Kachka suggested Ukraine could at least sign an accession treaty with the EU next year, even if ratification and other steps might take longer to implement.
But analysts and diplomats see little appetite for such bold moves, including von der Leyen's mooted revamp, known as "reverse enlargement" ‚Äãas it turns the process on its head ‚Äãby letting a country join before it ‚ÅÝhas met all the current criteria.
"The growing support for populist and anti-enlargement parties in several capitals makes governments cautious about being seen to accelerate a process they have not yet built public consent for," said Anastasia Pociumban, a research fellow at the German Council on Foreign Relations.
"The concept of reverse ‚Äãenlargement is dead," said one EU diplomat. "There is also no support for giving a concrete accession date."
A Western European official said: "Ukraine is just ‚Äãnot ready and has rampant ‚ÅÝcorruption."
Von der Leyen tried to temper expectations in Ukraine last week. She told Zelenskiy publicly that "dates by themselves are not possible" even as she praised the country‚Äôs reforms in the midst of war.
Nevertheless, the Commission is expected to continue to push for ways to get Kyiv into the EU in the next few years, arguing that membership would strengthen the bloc militarily amid fears Moscow ‚ÅÝmay attack ‚Äãan EU member next.
Some say a model of gradual integration, whereby aspiring members take part in more and more EU ‚Äãprogrammes and meetings before joining the club, may be more feasible.
"I think a full accession within the next few years remains unlikely," Corina Stratulat, associate director at the European Policy Centre, said of Ukraine's prospects.
"What seems more likely to happen instead ‚Äãis accelerated integration - single market access, energy, digital, transport - phased participation in EU programmes and policies."

item.title: Trump's Asian allies fear Iran war will sap defences against China
item.description:
TOKYO/TAIPEI/SYDNEY, March 3 (Reuters) - Japanese lawmakers reeling from attacks on Iran by the United States and Israel gathered on Monday at the ruling party's offices in Tokyo to question bureaucrats about evacuation plans, energy stocks and the legal ‚Äãbasis for U.S. action.
But one query posed at the closed-door meeting, described to Reuters by a politician who attended, reflected a deeper fear haunting Asia's corridors of power since ‚ÄåTrump's weekend attacks unleashed chaos in the Middle East.
How would the region respond to a hole left in its defences if Washington diverted ships and missiles it now uses to deter China?
The problem is urgent for Japan and South Korea, home to big U.S. military bases that help counter China's military flexing and nuclear-armed North Korea, as well as for democratic Taiwan, claimed by Beijing and armed by Washington.
"We hope this operation is fast, limited, and that resources can be promptly shifted back to Asia," said ‚ÄãChen Kuan-ting, a ruling party lawmaker in Taiwan who sits on its parliament's foreign affairs and defence committee.
A prolonged conflict could harm "stability and peace in the Indo-Pacific", said Chen, adding that Taipei ‚Äãmust prepare for Beijing to step up "coercion" while the United States is distracted.
Trump, who has said U.S. operations in the Middle East will last four or five ‚ÅÝweeks, but could be sustained far longer, plans to meet Chinese President Xi Jinping at the end of March, though Beijing has not confirmed his visit.
Taiwan is an internal matter for China and Beijing firmly opposes ‚Äãthe use of force to infringe on the sovereignty and security of other countries, Mao Ning, a spokesperson for China's foreign ministry said on Tuesday.
The U.S. State and Defense Departments did not immediately respond to a request ‚Äãfor comment for this report.
The Japanese politician who recounted Monday's questioning said a top foreign ministry official replied that Tokyo had sought assurances from Washington that it would not shift U.S. military assets.
About 40% of U.S. navy ships ready for operations are currently stationed around the Middle East, the Center for Strategic and International Studies said in a report last month.
These include an aircraft carrier, the Abraham Lincoln, and at least six missile destroyers based in the Pacific ports of California, Hawaii ‚Äãand Japan, the U.S. Naval Institute said on Monday.
The only U.S. carrier deployed in Asia, the George Washington, is undergoing maintenance at its base in the Japanese city of Yokosuka.
"The U.S. Navy is stretched thin," said ‚ÄãBryan Clark, a former U.S. defence official specialising in naval operations at the Hudson Institute.
If the war dragged on, there was a realistic possibility that the United States could draw down its naval strength in Asia to reinforce the Iran ‚Äåconflict, he added.
"The ‚ÅÝfleet ... is not sufficient to keep a steady presence in every theatre."
The Iran conflict is also depleting reserves of U.S. munitions, about which experts have long warned. The U.S. military has asked defence firms to step up production, but that could take several years.
That is a concern for the United States because rebuilding munitions reserves in the Indo-Pacific helps deter China from military action on Taiwan over the medium term, said a U.S. official, who sought anonymity as the matter is a sensitive one.
Japan has already faced delays in deliveries of hundreds of Tomahawk missiles ordered from the United States and could fall further behind schedule, said Jan van Tol, ‚Äãa senior fellow at the Center for Strategic and ‚ÄãBudgetary Assessments.
It is just three months since ‚ÅÝWashington unveiled a new security strategy that framed the Indo-Pacific as the key "geopolitical battleground", and made a top priority of deterring a conflict over Taiwan.
Since then, Trump has captured the leader of Venezuela in an audacious military strike, threatened to annex Greenland and teamed with Israel to launch the air war against Iran.
But while allies ‚Äãin Asia worry he may be taking his eye off the prize, some analysts say Beijing has little to cheer, at least for now.
By hitting ‚ÄãVenezuela and Iran, Trump weakened two ‚ÅÝof its allies that sent China streams of cheap oil, buoying its economy.
Some analysts have even suggested his military actions are part of a grand plan to enable the United States to focus on containing China.
But the longer Trump's entanglement in the Middle East persists, the more Beijing could start to benefit.
"The grand strategy is supposed to be 'contain Iran in the Middle East, then shift resources toward dealing with China,'" said a Japanese ruling party lawmaker, speaking ‚ÅÝon condition of ‚Äãanonymity.
"But the question is whether there will be enough resources left to shift."
China has exploited previous episodes of U.S. distraction, said ‚ÄãJennifer Parker, a former warfare officer with the Royal Australian Navy, pointing to its rapid militarisation of South China Sea islands as the U.S. pursued the war in Afghanistan.
"Beijing will be watching closely," added Parker, a non-resident fellow at the Sydney-based Lowy Institute think tank.

item.title: US debuts suicide drone in Iran after fast-tracked Pentagon procurement
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - The U.S. successfully debuted a low‚Äëcost suicide drone in combat ‚Äãin Iran just eight months after its Pentagon unveiling, as the U.S. pushes to accelerate weapons programs.
The Low-Cost ‚ÄåUncrewed Combat Attack System (LUCAS) drone, manufactured by Arizona's SpektreWorks, was showcased in July 2025 when Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth walked the Pentagon's inner courtyard with more than a dozen companies competing to supply the military with new equipment.
Drones have become central to modern warfare following their effective use in ‚Äãthe Ukraine war , including Iran-made Shahed systems flown by Russia that closely resemble the LUCAS. The sector is also among ‚Äãthe most fiercely competitive in the U.S. defense industry, with SpektreWorks vying for Pentagon contracts against major ‚ÅÝdefense primes and a wave of Silicon Valley‚Äìbacked startups such as Anduril, Shield AI and AeroVironment (AVAV.O) , opens new tab .
U.S. Central Command said LUCAS drones ‚Äãare modeled after the Shahed.
The rapid fielding of the LUCAS represents a departure from traditional Pentagon acquisition timelines, which typically ‚Äãspan years from initial development to operational deployment. Defense officials said the compressed timeline reflects lessons learned from observing drone warfare in Ukraine, where both sides have employed thousands of low-cost unmanned systems.
The LUCAS deployment comes as the Pentagon pushes to rapidly expand American industrial capacity for producing inexpensive, attritable ‚Äãdrones under the $1 billion Drone Dominance Program authorized in the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act of 2025."
The LUCAS drone uses an open ‚Äãarchitecture that allows different payloads and communications systems, and can be deployed either for strikes or as a target drone, according to company materials. ‚ÄåIt ‚ÅÝcan be launched from the ground or a truck. At about $35,000 each, it is far cheaper than the MQ‚Äë9 Reaper, which costs roughly $20 million to $40 million but is reusable and far more sophisticated.
The government owns the LUCAS design intellectual property, meaning multiple manufacturers could produce the system, though SpektreWorks currently holds manufacturing contracts.
SpektreWorks declined to comment for this story.
During ‚Äãits development at the Pentagon, the ‚ÄãLUCAS drone was paired with ‚ÅÝsatellite communications systems including Viasat‚Äôs MUSIC and SpaceX‚Äôs Starlink or Starshield, according to two sources familiar with the program. Reuters could not determine what connectivity systems are being used during current Iran ‚Äãoperations.
Neither SpaceX nor Viasat returned requests for comment.
A startup called Noda provides the software ‚Äãto control the ‚ÅÝdrones, known as an "orchestrator" that allows warfighters to control multiple autonomous systems, one of the sources familiar with the program said. Noda declined to comment.
Drone experts told Reuters the LUCAS design shares similarities with Iran's Shahed drone, which Tehran has supplied to Russia for use in ‚ÅÝUkraine. The ‚ÄãShahed is believed to be a copy of Israel's Harpy loitering munition, ‚Äãaccording to defense analysts. The Harpy design has been widely replicated by countries including China and Taiwan.
The LUCAS also bears resemblance to the Drone Anti-Radar (DAR), a loitering ‚Äãmunition jointly developed in the 1970s and 1980s.

item.title: US homeland chief Noem to face scrutiny on immigration crackdown at Senate hearing
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 3 (Reuters) - U.S. Department of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem is expected to face tough questions ‚Äãover President Donald Trump 's immigration crackdown during a Senate hearing on Tuesday, with funding for her department still stalled ‚Äådue to Democratic objections to its aggressive tactics.
Noem, appointed by the Republican president last year, also may field questions on other matters including possible threats to the United States after the U.S. attacks on Iran and reports of disorder within her department.
The former South Dakota governor has overseen Trump's immigration agenda, including ‚Äãthe deployment of thousands of masked federal agents to U.S. cities, where they have swept through neighborhoods in search of ‚Äãpossible immigration offenders and clashed with residents.
Noem is scheduled to testify before the Senate Judiciary Committee ‚ÅÝon Tuesday and the House of Representatives Judiciary Committee on Wednesday.
Trump has said mass deportations are needed after high levels of ‚Äãillegal immigration under his Democratic predecessor, Joe Biden, and has defended Noem's job performance.
Criticism of Noem intensified after federal immigration agents killed ‚Äãtwo U.S. citizens in Minneapolis in January. In both fatal shootings, Noem quickly called the victims "domestic terrorists" instead of waiting for a full investigation, leading to rebukes from both Democrats and Republicans as video evidence undercut her assertions about the events.
U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement is an agency within her department.
Democrats ‚Äãin Congress have refused to agree to new funding for her department without changes to immigration enforcement practices. Funding for the ‚Äã260,000-employee department lapsed last month , but most of its immigration enforcement and national security operations are deemed essential and continue.
House Democrats launched an effort in January ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝimpeach Noem, saying immigration agents under her leadership have violated the civil rights of U.S. citizens, that she had blocked congressional oversight of detention centers for migrants, and that she gave government contracts to Republican-affiliated firms and people connected to close associates of hers.
The impeachment effort is likely to fail, with the House controlled by Republicans.
During her testimony, Noem also could face questions concerning the actions ‚Äãof her top adviser, longtime ‚ÄãTrump ally Corey Lewandowski.
Citing sources, ‚ÅÝReuters reported last week that Lewandowski entered the cockpit uninvited on a U.S. government plane last year during a flight on which he later fired the pilot because Noem's blanket had been misplaced. ‚ÄãLewandowski said the facts as related by the sources were wrong but he did not respond ‚Äãto certain questions ‚ÅÝabout the incident.
Trump's immigration policies, once an area of strength, could now be a liability for Republicans trying to retain control of Congress in November midterm elections. A Reuters/Ipsos poll in February found that while a majority of Americans support deporting immigrants without legal status, about ‚ÅÝ60% think ‚Äãimmigration agents have gone too far.

item.title: Russia raises alarm over conflict's impact on Bushehr nuclear plant in Iran
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 3 (Reuters) - The Bushehr nuclear power plant in Iran ‚Äãis under threat as a ‚Äåresult of the escalating conflict, the head of Russian state nuclear corporation Rosatom ‚Äãsaid on Tuesday, according ‚Äãto the RIA state news agency.
"There ‚ÅÝis certainly a threat to ‚Äãthe plant," Likhachev was cited as ‚Äãsaying. "Explosions can be heard just kilometres away", he added, saying the plant itself was ‚Äãnot being targeted.
Rosatom said on ‚ÄãSaturday it had evacuated nearly 100 people from ‚ÄåIran, including ‚ÅÝemployees' children and non-essential staff, but other personnel have remained at the plant, which was built by ‚ÄãRussia in ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝIranian port city of Bushehr.
Likhachev said the next stage ‚Äãof evacuations involving 150 ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝ200 people from the plant would happen when the situation permits, according ‚ÅÝto ‚Äãcomments cited by ‚Äãthe Interfax news agency.

item.title: One in three EU women face violence, most cases unreported, survey finds
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 3 (Reuters) - Physical and sexual violence affects roughly a third of women in the European Union during their lifetime, but most incidents ‚Äãgo unreported, a survey revealed on Tuesday.
The results of the ‚Äåsurvey, conducted by the EU Agency for Fundamental Rights (FRA) and the European Institute for Gender Equality, showed that only 11.3% of women reported physical or sexual abuse by ‚Äãnon-partners to the police, and just 6.1% reported violence by intimate ‚Äãpartners.
Common reasons for not reporting violence included shame, self-blame, fear, ‚ÅÝand distrust in law enforcement. Limited awareness or access to support ‚Äãservices also emerged as contributing factors.
About 30.7% of women across the bloc said ‚Äãthey had experienced violence, a slight decrease from the 33% recorded in the first such survey in 2012. The study also highlighted other widespread forms of abuse, including psychological, ‚Äãeconomic, and online abuse.
"Violence against women is a fundamental rights violation," ‚ÄãFRA Director Sirpa Rautio said. "Member states have clear obligations to prevent violence, protect victims and ‚Äåensure ‚ÅÝaccess to justice, and these findings show there is still urgent work to do."
Prevalence rates varied significantly, ranging from 57.1% in Finland to 11.9% in Bulgaria.
Finland's Nordic neighbours, known for high gender equality, also showed high levels ‚Äãof violence, with ‚Äã52.5% of women ‚ÅÝin Sweden and 47.5% in Denmark experiencing violence, while lower-equality countries to the south show more complex reporting ‚Äãdynamics.
This disparity, termed the "Nordic paradox", could reflect actual variations in ‚Äãwomen's ‚ÅÝexperiences or contrasting reporting behaviors and different perceptions of violence related to sexual encounters between countries, according to researchers.
FRA's first survey on violence against women in ‚ÅÝthe ‚ÄãEU in 2012 showed the same paradox.
The new ‚Äãstudy drew on interviews with nearly 115,000 women aged 18 to 74 carried out between ‚ÄãSeptember 2020 and March 2024.

item.title: UN urges investigation into 'horrific' attack on Iran school
item.description:
GENEVA, March 3 (Reuters) - The U.N. human rights office urged the "forces" ‚Äãbehind an attack on ‚Äåa girls' school in Iran to investigate and share insights ‚Äãinto the "horrific" incident, without ‚Äãnaming them.
"The High Commissioner (Volker Turk) ‚ÅÝcalls for a prompt, ‚Äãimpartial and thorough investigation ‚Äãinto the circumstances of the attack. The onus is on the ‚Äãforces that carried out ‚Äãthe attack to investigate it," U.N. ‚Äåhuman ‚ÅÝrights office spokesperson Ravina Shamdasani told a Geneva press briefing, describing the incident as "horrific".
Secretary ‚Äãof ‚ÄãState ‚ÅÝMarco Rubio said U.S. forces "would not deliberately ‚Äãtarget a school", after ‚ÄãIranian ‚ÅÝstate media reported over 160 were killed on the first ‚ÅÝday ‚Äãof the U.S. ‚Äãand Israeli attacks on Iran.

item.title: UK planning to send warship to defend Cyprus military base, Times newspaper says
item.description:
LONDON, March 3 (Reuters) - Britain is planning ‚Äãto send a warship to ‚Äådefend its Royal Air Force Akrotiri base in Cyprus from Iranian ‚Äãattacks, the Times newspaper said ‚Äãon Tuesday.
An Iranian-made drone strike hit ‚ÅÝa runway at the Akrotiri ‚Äãbase in the early hours ‚Äãof Monday, and Britain has said UK assets were targeted by Iran.
The Times ‚Äãreport, citing three sources, said ‚Äãdefence minister John Healey had a meeting ‚Äåon ‚ÅÝTuesday with senior military figures, in which they discussed sending HMS Duncan to the region.
The ‚ÄãMinistry of ‚ÄãDefence ‚ÅÝdid not immediately respond to a request for ‚Äãcomment on the report.
Earlier ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝTuesday , the semi-official Cyprus News Agency (CNA) said France plans to send ‚ÅÝanti-missile ‚Äãand anti-drone systems ‚Äãto Cyprus.

item.title: Turkey says it's engaging with all sides to end Iran war, resume diplomacy
item.description:
ANKARA, March 3 (Reuters) - Turkey is engaging with all parties to find a way to end the war in Iran and return to negotiations, Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said, adding that Ankara ‚Äãwas also in talks with Oman on the matter as the Gulf nation works ‚Äåfor the same goal.
The United States and Israel launched strikes against Iran on Saturday. The war quickly widened with Tehran striking Gulf states that host U.S. bases and Israel attacking Iranian-backed Hezbollah targets in Lebanon.
Turkey, a NATO member ‚Äãand neighbour of Iran, had for weeks urged Washington and Tehran to reach an ‚Äãagreement during their rounds of negotiations over Iran's nuclear programme and missile capabilities, ‚ÅÝwarning that the region could not handle any more destabilisation.
