One person who has actively supported Hezbollah told Reuters that they had attended a rally organized by the group on Sunday evening to protest against ‚ÅÝKhamenei's killing. They ‚Äãreturned home, convinced that was the most Hezbollah would do.
"We were frankly surprised when the Israelis said rockets had been launched from ‚ÄãLebanon and we kept waiting for Hezbollah's denial. But the real surprise came when Hezbollah claimed responsibility," the person said.
"Frankly, we are not in favour of starting a war with unforeseen consequences. Absolutely not. And whoever made this decision is wrong. Look at how scattered the people ‚Äãare in the streets, not knowing where to go. Does this community, which has made so much sacrifice, deserve this?"

item.title: Turkey says NATO defences destroyed missile from Iran headed toward its airspace
item.description:
ANKARA, March 4 (Reuters) - NATO air and missile defence systems stationed in the eastern Mediterranean ‚Äãdestroyed a ballistic missile fired from Iran as ‚Äåit headed into Turkish airspace after passing Syria and Iraq, the Turkish Defence Ministry said on Wednesday.
The incident marks the first time ‚Äãthat NATO member Turkey, Iran's neighbour to the ‚Äãnorthwest, has been drawn into the expanding conflict ‚ÅÝbetween Iran and the U.S. and Israel that now ‚Äãinvolves several countries in the region.
"We warn all parties to ‚Äãrefrain from actions that would lead to further escalation of conflict in the region. In this context, we will continue to consult ‚Äãwith NATO and our other allies," the ministry said, ‚Äãadding there were no casualties or injuries.
"All necessary steps to defend our ‚Äåterritory ‚ÅÝand airspace will be taken resolutely and without hesitation. We remind all parties that we reserve the right to respond to any hostile actions against our country," it ‚Äãadded.
Two other initial ‚Äãstatements by ‚ÅÝsenior Turkish officials about the incident did not mention NATO's Article 4, which states ‚Äãthat allies will "consult together whenever, in the opinion ‚Äãof ‚ÅÝany of them, the territorial integrity, political independence or security" of a member is threatened.
Ankara said debris from the air ‚ÅÝdefence ‚Äãmissile that intercepted the ballistic missile ‚Äãfell in the Dortyol district of Hatay province in southeastern Turkey.

item.title: Italy likely to send military aid to Gulf states, sources say
item.description:
ROME, March 4 (Reuters) - Italy's government is likely to provide military aid, including an air defence ‚Äåsystem, to Gulf nations that have requested help with equipment in the face of Iranian air strikes, two sources said on Wednesday.
The sources, who asked not to be named, said that while a final decision had ‚Äãnot yet been made, Italy would probably send one of its SAMP/T batteries ‚Äãthat can intercept ballistic missiles. They would not say which country would ‚ÅÝreceive the system, or which battery Rome was considering redeploying.
Attacks by Israeli and U.S. ‚Äãforces against Iran have prompted Tehran to launch retaliatory strikes on ports, cities and oil facilities throughout ‚Äãthe Gulf, a vital energy-producing region. Italy is not particularly reliant on energy supplies from the Gulf, but its energy minister said on Wednesday the country could reactivate some coal-fired power plants if the conflict leads ‚Äãto an energy crisis.
On Monday, Defence Minister Guido Crosetto said Rome had received requests ‚Äãfor air defence and anti-drone systems from the Gulf, including the SAMP/T, a Franco-Italian battery.
A third source said ‚ÄåItaly ‚ÅÝcould also provide anti-drone systems, which could be transferred quickly, although a technical assessment was still under way.
Italy is already supplying the SAMP/T to Ukraine to help its war effort against the Russian invasion, and the third source ruled out taking away from Kyiv's air defences to ‚Äãsupport the Gulf states.
When ‚Äãasked about the ‚ÅÝpossible supply of defence tools in response to the latest Middle East conflict, Cabinet Undersecretary Alfredo Mantovano told an event in Rome that ‚Äãthe government was "evaluating requests".
As the conflict widens to the Mediterranean, Britain, France ‚Äãand Greece said ‚ÅÝon Tuesday that they would send air defence forces to Cyprus after drones struck the Royal Air Force Akrotiri base on the island.
Italy also hosts U.S. military bases on its territory.
Foreign Minister ‚ÅÝAntonio Tajani ‚Äãsaid late on Tuesday that Rome had not yet ‚Äãreceived any requests to use them for operations against Iran, and would evaluate any requests if they were to ‚Äãarrive.

item.title: Iran intelligence operatives signalled openness to talks with CIA to end war, NYT reports
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Operatives from Iran's Ministry of Intelligence signalled openness to the U.S. Central ‚ÄãIntelligence Agency (CIA) to talks on ending ‚Äåthe war , the New York Times reported on Wednesday, citing officials briefed on the matter.
The ‚Äãoffer was made through an unnamed ‚Äãcountry's spy agency, the NYT said, ‚ÅÝciting Middle Eastern officials and officials ‚Äãfrom a Western nation who spoke on ‚Äãcondition of anonymity.
The White House and the CIA did not immediately respond to a request for ‚Äãcomment.
Officials in Washington are sceptical as ‚Äãto whether Iran or the Trump administration is ‚Äåreally ‚ÅÝready for an "off-ramp", at least in the short term, the report added.
Iran's ambassador to the United Nations in Geneva on ‚ÄãTuesday ruled out ‚Äãfor ‚ÅÝnow any negotiations with the United States, days after the ‚ÄãU.S. and Israel launched joint strikes ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝhis country.
President Donald Trump said on Tuesday that Tehran wanted to talk but it ‚ÅÝwas ‚Äãtoo late, as the ‚ÄãUnited States continued its military operation against Iran.

item.title: Two men go on trial in London accused of spying for Hong Kong and China
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - Two men went on trial in London on Wednesday accused by British ‚Äãprosecutors of carrying out hostile surveillance on well-known pro-democracy dissidents for Hong Kong and, ultimately, China.
Hong ‚ÄåKong was under British rule for 156 years before reverting to Chinese sovereignty in 1997, and there have been growing tensions between the two nations over a sweeping national security crackdown since sometimes violent pro-democracy protests swept the territory in 2019.
Chung Biu Yuen, 65, ‚Äãand Chi Leung Wai, 38, both dual Chinese and British nationals, deny charges of assisting a foreign intelligence ‚Äãservice between December 2023 and May 2024 and conducting "foreign interference" by forcing entry into a ‚ÅÝresidential address on May 1.
Prosecutor Duncan Atkinson told London's Old Bailey court the two men had been tasked to ‚Äãcarry out "shadow policing operations on behalf of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region and thereby the People's Republic of China".
Atkinson said this included targeting dissidents now resident in Britain for whom the Hong Kong government had issued bounties of about 100,000 pounds ($133,710) for information leading to their whereabouts or capture.
"Messaging on Mr Yuen's phone between him and Wai indicated that surveillance ‚Äãon pro-democracy protester Nathan Law had been ongoing since 2021," Atkinson said. "The evidence includes messages regarding surveillance against Nathan ‚ÄãLaw and discussion around his associates badmouthing Hong Kong."
Yuen, a retired Hong Kong police officer, worked at Hong Kong's Economic and Trade ‚ÄåOffice (HKETO) ‚ÅÝin London. Wai, known as Peter Wai, worked as a UK Border Force officer and as a volunteer part-time officer for the City of London Police, Atkinson said.
The two men are also accused of gathering intelligence on behalf of HKETO about Monica Kwong who left Hong Kong in December 2023 after being accused of fraud by her employer Tina ‚ÄãZou, Atkinson said.
The defendants carried ‚Äãout surveillance on Kwong's ‚ÅÝresidence in Pontefract, northern England, and used deception and then force to attempt to get into her home, Atkinson said.
Wai is also accused of misusing his job with the ‚ÄãBorder Force to gain access to the interior ministry's computer system.
The Chinese Embassy in ‚ÄãLondon has accused ‚ÅÝBritain of fabricating the charges against the men and said it had no right to interfere in Hong Kong's affairs.
In January, British Prime Minister Keir Starmer visited China and held talks with President Xi Jinping as part of an effort to ‚ÅÝimprove bilateral ‚Äãrelations, which have been strained in recent years amid mutual accusations ‚Äãof spying.
Starmer is seeking to strike a difficult balance between warning of the security threat Beijing poses while trying to boost economic ties. His government ‚Äãrecently approved China's plans to build in London its largest embassy in Europe.
($1 = 0.7479 pounds)

item.title: Heavy shelling, explosions spark fear along Pakistan-Afghanistan border
item.description:
LAL PUR, Afghanistan/PESHAWAR, Pakistan, March 4 (Reuters) - People living along Pakistan's border with Afghanistan ‚Äãsaid they were considering fleeing their homes because of heavy shelling and explosions as fighting between troops from ‚Äåboth sides entered a seventh day on Wednesday.
The South Asian allies-turned-foes have engaged in their worst fighting in years following Pakistani airstrikes on major Afghan cities last week, increasing volatility in a region also on edge over U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran .
Islamabad has said its airstrikes, which have at times directly ‚Äãtargeted the Taliban government, are aimed at ending Afghan support for militants carrying out attacks on Pakistan. The Taliban has ‚Äãdenied aiding militant groups.
Residents of towns and villages in Pakistan's ‚ÅÝnorthwest said fighting between border forces starts in the evenings, placing their homes in the line of fire, often at sunset ‚Äãwhen families are breaking their fast in the holy month of Ramadan.
"There is complete silence in the day, but the moment we ‚Äãsit for iftar dinner, the two sides start shelling," Farid Khan Shinwari from Landi Kotal, a town near the Torkham border crossing, told Reuters.
"We open our fast in extremely difficult situations, as you never know when a shell can hit your house."
Residents in the town and nearby villages said there ‚Äãhad been heavy shelling and some explosions heard in the past few days, prompting many to flee their homes.
On the other side ‚Äãof the border, Afghans shared similar stories of skirmishes and families fleeing their homes.
Hundreds had been displaced to an open dirt field under makeshift ‚Äåtents, while ‚ÅÝothers had no shelter at all. Officials say around 1,500 families have fled their homes.
Fighting along the 2,600-km (1,615-mile) border has ebbed and flowed over the week-long conflict, with both sides saying they have inflicted heavy losses on the other country and gained ground in the fighting.
Reuters has been unable to verify these accounts.
Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan told Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz ‚ÄãSharif that Ankara would help reinstate ‚Äãa ceasefire, the Turkish Presidency ‚ÅÝsaid on Tuesday, as other countries that had offered to mediate have since been hit by the conflict in the Gulf .
On Wednesday, both countries reported exchanges of heavy fire, with Afghanistan's defence ministry ‚Äãsaying Taliban forces shot down a Pakistani drone and captured seven border posts.
A spokesperson for the ‚Äãministry said 110 ‚ÅÝcivilians, including 65 women and children, had been killed since the fighting began and another 123 were wounded. The United Nations mission for Afghanistan has listed 42 deaths so far.
Pakistan's Information Minister Attaullah Tarar disputed both figures, saying: "Pakistan exercises great care in only targeting terrorists and ‚ÅÝsupport infrastructure. ‚ÄãNo civilian structures have been targeted."
On Saturday, Pakistan struck "ammunition and critical equipment" at ‚Äãthe Bagram air base north of Kabul, Tarar said, a key American command centre through the 20-year Afghan war.

item.title: Trump's Hormuz shipping plan is too little, too late in race to avert energy shock
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump‚Äôs current plan to revive shipping through the Strait of Hormuz via financial guarantees and security assistance will require a Herculean international effort.
Even if successful, it will likely offer only limited relief as time quickly runs out to avert the worst global economic fallout from the closure of this vital energy artery.
Trump said ‚Äãon Tuesday he had ordered the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation to provide political risk insurance and financial guarantees for maritime trade in the Gulf. He also said the U.S. Navy ‚Äåcould begin escorting vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow shipping lane between Iran and Oman through which around a fifth of global oil and gas supplies normally passes.
The measures are Washington‚Äôs attempt to ease pressure on global energy markets after traffic through the Strait effectively ground to a halt on Saturday following the start of the joint U.S.-Israeli aerial bombardment of Iran.
Tehran retaliated by striking neighbouring countries, including energy infrastructure, forcing the shutdown of Qatar‚Äôs liquefied natural gas production and Saudi Arabia‚Äôs largest oil refinery .
At least four tankers were ‚Äãalso targeted in or near Hormuz, prompting many ship insurers and charterers to suspend transit in and out of the Gulf. The closure triggered a surge in oil and gas prices ‚Äì with Brent ‚Äãrising above $84 per barrel at one point, the highest level since July 2024 ‚Äì and sent stock markets tumbling, particularly in Asia, as investors braced for a severe economic ‚ÅÝshock.
Yet the U.S. response is unlikely to reassure shippers under current conditions.
Tanker freight rates have soared in recent days, with costs on many routes hitting record highs. Chartering a crude carrier capable of carrying 2 million ‚Äãbarrels of oil from the Gulf to Asia now costs around $30 million - roughly 5% of the cargo‚Äôs value at current prices and nearly five times its level at the start of the year.
But reducing costs would ‚Äãdo little to address fears that vessels could still be attacked. U.S. naval escorts would certainly lower the risk, but they are unlikely to offer full protection against Iran's extensive use of drones, missiles and fast-attack boats.
This would not be the first time Washington has intervened to secure shipping lanes in the region.
During the "Tanker War" phase of the Iran‚ÄìIraq conflict in the late 1980s, the U.S. escorted and protected Kuwaiti oil tankers under Operation Earnest Will to deter Iranian attacks.
The scale of the challenge today, however, ‚Äãis much bigger.
Oil and gas exports from the region have nearly doubled since then to roughly 20 million bpd last year. Qatar, now the world‚Äôs second‚Äëlargest LNG producer, exported around 80 million metric tons last year - about ‚Äãa fifth of global demand. In the 1980s, it wasn‚Äôt even a player in the global energy markets.
Securing such enormous volumes of oil, gas and tankers would be a formidable task and would almost certainly require assistance from other countries' ‚Äånavies.
And, even more ‚ÅÝimportantly, organising such an effort would take days, if not weeks.
Time, however, is running out for both producers and consumers.
The Hormuz blockade is already forcing Gulf producers to curb output. Iraq cut production on Tuesday by more than 1.1 million bpd, roughly a quarter of its total output, due to a lack of storage capacity. Officials said output could fall by more than 3 million bpd within days if the disruption persists.
Other producers face similar constraints.
Saudi Arabia, the world‚Äôs largest crude exporter, shipped around 7 million bpd in February and is now diverting some output to the Red Sea port of Yanbu via a pipeline with a capacity of 5 million ‚Äãbpd. But Yanbu‚Äôs export capacity is limited to no ‚Äãmore than 2 million bpd, so the kingdom has ‚ÅÝbeen forced to place large volumes into onshore storage.
And Saudi Arabia already holds about 82 million barrels of crude in onshore storage, around 56% of capacity, according to Kayrros data.
The United Arab Emirates, which exported about 3.3 million bpd last month, can divert up to 1.5 million bpd through a pipeline that bypasses Hormuz. But, again, ‚Äãthat means tapping storage, which is around 40% full with about 34 million barrels already being held, Kayrros said.
As a result, Saudi Arabia, the UAE and ‚ÄãKuwait could see storage fill ‚ÅÝwithin days, forcing deeper production cuts.
Pressure is also intensifying on consumers.
Asian refiners heavily dependent on Middle Eastern oil are struggling to replace supplies and are likely to cut operating rates. Two Chinese refineries have already reduced runs, while India has curtailed gas supplies to its industrial base due to shortages.
The shock is rippling throughout Asian financial markets. South Korea‚Äôs KOSPI index fell 18% so far this week, partly on fears that the country‚Äôs vast petrochemical and manufacturing sectors - both highly ‚ÅÝdependent on Middle ‚ÄãEastern energy - could be disrupted.
The key question remains how long the war will last. Trump has indicated it could drag on for ‚Äãseveral weeks, but the global energy system may not be able to wait that long ‚Äì even if his plans to reopen Hormuz prove successful.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid ‚Äãdaily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Morning Bid: Seoul-sapping selloff
item.description:
March 4 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
The ongoing Middle East eruption shocked Asia's stock markets on Wednesday, with South Korea's Kospi plummeting 12% for its worst day ever and Japan's ‚ÄãNikkei and Taiwan's benchmark each losing about 4%.
The Korean won hit its weakest in 17 years, while Seoul and several other Asian ‚Äåcenters were forced to suspend trading periodically thanks to the scale of the selloff.
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why gold lost some of its safe‚Äëhaven shine this week.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days ‚Äãa week.
Asia's big manufacturing economies are heavily exposed to imported energy products from the Middle East, so both the price rise and ‚Äãsupply uncertainty caused by the conflict are alarming.
However, there were tentative signs that markets elsewhere are taking a breather, as ‚ÅÝinvestors await further developments. Both U.S. and global Brent crude oil prices were up another 3% but held below Tuesday's 8-month and 19-month highs, respectively.
Europe's stocks ‚Äãpopped up about 0.5% in what looked like a pause after two days of heavy selling. U.S. stock futures were marginally higher too. And the dollar's rise flattened ‚Äãout for the most part, even though government bond yields continued to push higher.
Gold and precious metals, unexpected losers this week during the geopolitical shock, regained some poise on Wednesday as the dash for cash abated somewhat.
President Trump announced plans to provide shipping insurance and possible naval support for energy supplies to exit an effectively closed Gulf - and that may be helping ‚Äãat the margins, although these moves could take some time to have an impact. World markets asking when this energy blockage will abate are having to ‚Äãthink in weeks rather than days.
The conflict in Iran and across the region is impossible to predict. Many are now focusing on who's set to take over as Supreme Leader ‚Äåfollowing the ‚ÅÝkilling of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei last weekend. Some in markets took heart from a New York Times story that said Tehran officials had made a secret outreach , opens new tab to Washington over the weekend on how to end the conflict.
But for investors who think the coast will be clear once Gulf tensions ease, there are plenty of other things to worry about. For one, there‚Äôs rising concern about private credit funds run by the likes of Blackstone and BlackRock.
Today's calendar sees more routine issues ‚Äãback on the radar, with the ‚ÄãADP's private sector jobs report and ‚ÅÝISM's service sector survey both coming out. The former may be looked at more closely in light of the big U.S. payrolls report due on Friday.
Chart of the day
European gas prices have soared this week on the energy supply disruption ‚Äãin the Middle East and Qatar in particular, hitting their highest levels in three years - almost 20% higher ‚Äãthan they were this ‚ÅÝtime last year.
As the Iran conflict unfolds, Europe is emerging from the winter with gas storage levels running well below five-year averages. The EU on Wednesday told member states it does not see any immediate impact on the security of natural gas supply.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. February ADP employment figures (8:15 AM EST), February S&P Global/ISM ‚ÅÝservices PMIs (9:45-10:00 ‚ÄãAM EST)
* U.S. Federal Reserve issues latest Beige Book
* U.S. corporate earnings: Broadcom
Want to receive the ‚ÄãMorning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the ‚Äãauthor. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Hormuz shutdown worsens after US hits Iranian warship; tankers stranded for fifth day
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - The U.S.‚ÄìIran war widened on Wednesday after a U.S. strike hit an Iranian warship off ‚ÄãSri Lanka, deepening a crisis that has paralysed shipping through the Strait of Hormuz for a fifth day and choked off ‚Äåvital Middle East oil and gas flows.
The U.S. submarine strike on the Iranian vessel came as U.S. President Donald Trump pledged to provide insurance and navy escorts to ships exporting oil and gas from the Middle East in a bid to contain soaring energy prices.
At least 200 ships, including oil and liquefied natural gas tankers as well as cargo ships, remained at anchor in ‚Äãopen waters off the coast of major Gulf producers including Iraq, Saudi Arabia and Qatar, according to Reuters estimates based on ship-tracking data from ‚Äãthe MarineTraffic platform.
Hundreds of other vessels remained outside Hormuz unable to reach ports, shipping data showed. The waterway is a ‚ÅÝkey artery for around a fifth of the world's oil and LNG supply.
The Maltese-flagged container ship Safeen Prestige was also damaged by a projectile as it ‚Äãsailed toward the northern end of the Strait of Hormuz earlier, prompting the crew to abandon ship, shipping sources said.
Qatar suspended its gas output and Iraq cut ‚Äãits oil production as both ran out of storage for gas and oil, unable to load it in tankers. Saudi Arabia, the UAE and Kuwait were also struggling to load oil but it was not yet clear if they cut output.
Despite the gridlock, a rare voyage took place on Tuesday when the Suezmax tanker Pola sailed through ‚Äãthe Strait of Hormuz to the UAE to load crude, according to industry sources and LSEG ship-tracking data.
The Pola had switched off its AIS transponder ‚Äãlate on March 2 as it approached the Strait and reappeared the next day off Abu Dhabi.
Trump on Tuesday said he had instructed the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation to ‚Äåprovide political-risk ‚ÅÝinsurance and financial guarantees for maritime trade in the Gulf.
"No matter what, the United States will ensure the free flow of energy to the world," he wrote in a social media post.
Oil prices dipped on Wednesday, having risen for the fourth day and gaining 12% since the war started on Saturday, as U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran disrupted Middle East supplies. The pace of gains slowed from past sessions.
On Wednesday, Goldman Sachs raised its forecast for Brent crude oil in the second quarter by $10 ‚Äãto $76 per barrel. It also raised its ‚Äãprediction for WTI by $9 to $71 ‚ÅÝper barrel.
The bank said it sees longer-than-expected disruption to exports of oil and gas through the Strait of Hormuz and damage to oil production facilities as risks. It also assumes that low oil flows through Hormuz will lead to large ‚Äãdeclines in OECD inventories and Middle East production in March.
"Providing protection for all tankers operating in areas currently threatened ‚Äãby Iran is unrealistic ‚ÅÝas this would require a very high number of warships and other military assets," said Jakob Larsen, chief safety and security officer with shipping association BIMCO.
Some Asian refiners face output cuts because they are unable to get prompt replacement cargoes from their suppliers in the Gulf due to the shipping standstill, four traders ‚ÅÝand three ‚Äãanalysts said.
Asia sources 60% of its oil from the Middle East, leaving the region vulnerable ‚Äãto the disruptions. Refiners in Indonesia and Japan are sourcing more oil from the U.S. to replace the shortfall. India will consider buying more from Russia, sources at two companies have said.
Saudi Aramco's ‚Äãlargest domestic refinery and key crude export terminal Ras Tanura was struck on Wednesday , four sources said.

item.title: Iran war breaks UN Charter, strike on school shocking, UN probe says
item.description:
GENEVA, March 4 (Reuters) - An independent United Nations probe investigating rights violations in Iran condemned ‚Äãon Wednesday attacks by Israel and the United States on Iran as well ‚Äåas Tehran's retaliatory strikes across the region, saying they violated the UN Charter.
The United Nations Charter bans the use of force against the territorial integrity or political independence ‚Äãof any state.
"These attacks, which were followed by Iran's retaliatory strikes ‚Äãacross the region, run counter to the UN Charter," the U.N. Independent ‚ÅÝInternational Fact-Finding Mission on Iran said in a statement.
It also expressed deep ‚Äãshock over a strike that hit the Shajareh Tayyebeh girls school in Minab ‚Äãin southern Iran on Saturday, the first day of the U.S. and Israeli attacks.
Most of the victims appear to have been schoolgirls aged seven to 12, it said.
Earlier on ‚ÄãWednesday a separate U.N. panel of experts said more than 160 children were ‚Äãkilled, citing reports.
The U.N. fact-finding probe said the Iranian population was now caught between ‚Äåa large-scale ‚ÅÝmilitary campaign that may go on for weeks and a government in Tehran which has a long record of human rights abuses.
Tens of thousands of people were detained and face torture and the death penalty, the U.N. ‚Äãprobe said, following ‚Äãa brutal crackdown on ‚ÅÝprotests that began on December 28, 2025, in response to the country's economic crisis.
It said protesters currently detained in ‚Äãprisons could be put at risk from any U.S.-Israeli strikes. ‚ÄãA ‚ÅÝBritish couple jailed in Iran described on Tuesday explosions shaking Evin prison where they are being held and damage to their wing as the conflict intensifies.
The statement ‚ÅÝsaid the ‚Äãkilling of dozens of Iranian officials - who ‚Äãhave included Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei - in the U.S.-Israeli airstrikes was not an acceptable means to ‚Äãdeliver justice under international law.

item.title: Swedish fighter jets patrol Iceland in show of force over Arctic
item.description:
KEFLAVIK, Iceland, March 4 (Reuters) - Swedish fighter jets are patrolling the skies around Iceland for the first time, as NATO allies ‚Äãtry to show both U.S. President Donald Trump and his Russian counterpart Vladimir Putin that they are serious about protecting ‚Äåthe Arctic.
Sweden has sent six of its Saab Gripen fighter jets, the crown jewel of its defence industry, as part of NATO mission Arctic Sentry.
The mission is seeking to strengthen NATO's presence in the strategically important Arctic region as part of an effort to defuse severe tensions within the alliance prompted by Trump's push for the ‚ÄãU.S. to acquire Greenland.
Trump has forced NATO allies to sharply increase military spending, including in the Arctic. He has been ‚Äãparticularly unimpressed by Denmark's military presence on Greenland, which he mockingly described as "two dog sleds".
The ‚ÅÝSwedish fighters are mainly on the lookout for Russian long-range bombers that can take off from air bases on the Kola Peninsula, ‚Äãwhich is home to Russia's Northern Fleet and also has nuclear missile silos.
"Iceland as well as all of the Arctic would be important (in case ‚Äãof a conflict) because that's the warning chain for attacks against the U.S. or Canada across the Arctic," said Major General Frode Arnfinn Kristoffersen, deputy chief of staff, operations, at NATO Joint Force Command Norfolk.
"Russia sees the Arctic as vital to becoming a great power and has also built up new Arctic bases ‚Äãand opened up former Soviet military sites, including deep-water ports and airfields," he said.
Russia has repeatedly dismissed claims it is seeking to take over ‚ÄãGreenland, or is a threat in the Arctic, saying they are false "horror stories" made up by Western leaders to justify their own behaviour.
Keflavik ‚ÄåAir Base, ‚ÅÝbuilt during World War Two and up until 2006 an American base, is home to the NATO air policing missions that have been done periodically since 2008.
The cold and windy conditions are the same as those on Greenland and the rest of the Arctic.
"We have experiences from operating in this kind of harsh environment...so for us, it's quite natural to also be able to operate here in Iceland," said Major ‚ÄãGeneral Jonas Wikman, Sweden's Air Force ‚Äãchief.
The JAS 39 Gripen is ‚ÅÝa fourth-generation light single-engine supersonic multi-role aircraft, meaning it can do air-to-ground, air-to-surface, and air-to-air operations.
"It's perfectly suitable for this weather. It's built in Sweden for the Swedish climate, and this is basically what we ‚Äãhave back in Sweden," said Lieutenant Colonel Robin Arvidsson, fighter pilot and squadron commander in the Swedish ‚ÄãAir Force.
It has ‚ÅÝa low logistical footprint and can be refuelled and rearmed by one officer and four conscripts in as little as 10 minutes.
While NATO allies have rushed to increase the alliance's presence in the Arctic, some analysts and politicians have questioned the rationale behind the build-up, saying Russia does ‚ÅÝnot currently ‚Äãpose a threat to Greenland.
"This is very dangerous because it leads to an action-reaction ‚Äãcycle," said Pavel Devyatkin, senior associate at Washington-based think tank, The Arctic Institute.
"There's a very high chance of an accidental incident, of an escalation," he said. "These moves from ‚ÄãSweden and from NATO are basically appeasing President Trump," he said.

item.title: US says Iran firing fewer missiles, US strikes to expand inland
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - Iran is firing fewer missiles than it did at ‚Äåthe start of the war on Saturday, the top U.S. general said on Wednesday, as he argued Iran's ‚Äãmilitary capabilities were greatly diminished as the ‚ÄãUnited States expands its strikes inside Iran.
"We will ‚ÅÝnow begin to expand inland striking progressively ‚Äãdeeper into Iranian territory, and creating additional freedom ‚Äãof maneuver for US forces," General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, told a news briefing at ‚Äãthe Pentagon.
U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said ‚Äãthe United States was winning in its war against Iran, ‚Äåeven ‚ÅÝas he lamented six U.S. troops killed so far in the conflict. He added the United States would outlast Iran.
Hegseth confirmed that the U.S. military ‚Äãsank an ‚ÄãIranian warship ‚ÅÝoff the coast of Sri Lanka, calling it the first sinking of ‚Äãan enemy ship by torpedo since World ‚ÄãWar ‚ÅÝTwo.
The U.S. sinking was first reported by Reuters.
"An American submarine sunk an Iranian warship that thought it was ‚ÅÝsafe ‚Äãin international waters. Instead, it ‚Äãwas sunk by a torpedo. Quiet death," Hegseth said.

item.title: Iran's Revolutionary Guards take wartime lead, ensuring harder line, sources say
item.description:
DUBAI, March 4 (Reuters) - Iran‚Äôs Revolutionary Guards have tightened their grip on wartime decision‚Äëmaking despite the loss of top commanders, senior sources say, driving a hardline strategy that is propelling Tehran's drone‚Äëand‚Äëmissile campaign across the region.
Anticipating the decapitation of their leadership, the Guards had already delegated far down the ranks before Saturday's U.S.-Israeli attack , a resilience-building ‚Äãstrategy that could also risk miscalculation or a wider war with mid-ranking officers empowered to attack neighbouring states . On Wednesday, Iran fired on Turkey , a NATO nation.
Inside Iran, the Guards' central role at all levels of the ‚Äåsystem and draconian approach to security may also make it harder for protests to erupt , undermining any U.S. or Israeli hopes their attack will spur an uprising and regime change.
The choice of the next supreme leader, after Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's death on Saturday, could further cement their role, said Kasra Aarabi, head of research on the Guards at United Against Nuclear Iran, a U.S.-based policy organisation.
Khamenei's son Mojtaba , widely seen as a likely candidate, has very close ties with the Guards , exercising significant control over them and enjoying extensive support, including from more radical junior ranks.
"If the conflict suddenly stops and ‚Äãthe regime survives, we can be certain the Guards will have an even more important role," said Aarabi.
Reuters spoke to six Iranian and regional sources with close knowledge of the Guards for this ‚Äãarticle, with all confirming they had taken a far greater role in the hierarchy since the war began on Saturday and were now involved in every big decision.
A security official close to ‚ÅÝthe Guards said the Guards' new head, Ahmad Vahidi, was present in every high-ranking meeting and that its overriding objective was always the survival of Iran's Islamic revolutionary system and its goals.
Deputy defence minister and Guardsman Reza Talaeinik spelled out the ‚Äãelite force's efforts to build resilience in a television interview on Tuesday, saying each figure in the command structure had named successors stretching three ranks down ready to replace them.
"The role of each unit and section has been organised in such a way ‚Äãthat if any commander is killed, a successor immediately takes their place," he said.
Israeli strikes last year killed the Guards' overall head and the heads of their intelligence, aerospace and economic units. On Saturday an airstrike killed the latest Guards head, Mohammad Pakpour.
Decentralisation has been part of the Guards' doctrine in case of attack for nearly 20 years and was developed after watching the collapse of Iraqi forces during the U.S.-led invasion in 2003, Aarabi said.
"The whole idea was to decentralise so that if one particular province came under attack, it could defend itself and sustain the ‚Äãregime's authority and rule," he said.
Crucially, the plan was designed to ensure that the Guards could continue to act as both the main spearhead of Iran's military response to external attacks, and as the ‚Äãenforcers of internal security inside the Islamic Republic, he added.
The approach appears to be holding for now, though sustained attacks that continue to pick off both senior and more junior Guards commanders could eventually test the Guards' ability to maintain strategic coherence.
To be sure, the Guards ‚Äåare not an ‚ÅÝentirely homogeneous unit, with their own factional rivalries, personal disputes and differences over the group's role. But one of the sources said they are more "united than ever when Iran is under attack".
There may also be signs, five days into the Israeli and U.S. strikes, of the command structure starting to degrade, said Aarabi, pointing to what he called increasingly wild attacks on civilian targets in Gulf monarchies.
How far that may also reflect a deliberate strategy to show the attack on Iran was a mistake with global implications is not certain.
Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi said Iran's response to the attack had already been planned.
"These units are operating based on general instructions given to them in advance, rather than direct, real-time command from the current political leadership," he told Al Jazeera.
While ‚Äãthe Guards are now involved in almost every strategic decision ‚Äãtaken in Iran - even beyond the central role they ‚ÅÝheld before the war - they can also count on a surviving political leadership in which the three top men are former Guards members.
The Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps was founded soon after Iran's 1979 revolution to defend the new republic against both internal and external foes, and as a counterweight to the regular armed forces.
Answering directly to the supreme leader, it has ‚Äãemerged as a state-within-a-state, combining military power, an intelligence network and economic might all focused on maintaining the survival of Iran's Islamic system of power.
That role was put to ‚Äãthe test when Iraq invaded months ‚ÅÝafter the revolution, unleashing an eight-year war of attrition that was a formative experience for many of the current generation of Iranian leaders.
Senior Iranian figures who served with the Guards in the war include the three non-clerics occupying the most critical positions in Iran since Khamenei's death.
President Masoud Pezeshkian was a battlefield surgeon, Parliament Speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf fought on frontlines before heading the Guards' air force unit, while Ali Larijani, Khamenei's top adviser, was a staff officer behind the lines.
From the early 2000s, as the wartime generation began moving into ‚ÅÝmore leadership positions ‚Äãand as Iran's long confrontation with the West accelerated, the role of the Guards in the Iranian state also began to increase.
The Guards were put ‚Äãin charge of Iran's nuclear programme, a project Tehran has always maintained is for purely peaceful purposes, but which Western countries believe is cover for building an atomic bomb.
As sanctions imposed over the nuclear project bit, the Guards took a role in the economy, their construction arm Khatam al-Anbia winning major contracts ‚Äãincluding in the all-important energy sector.
The Guards increasingly also served as the conduit to Shi'ite proxies across the Middle East, while their volunteer paramilitary, the Basij, was used to crush internal unrest.

item.title: At least 80 killed in US strike on Iranian warship, Sri Lankan minister says
item.description: March 4 (Reuters) - At least ‚Äã80 were ‚Äåpeople killed ‚Äãin a ‚ÄãU.S. submarine strike ‚ÅÝon ‚Äãan Iranian ‚Äãwarship in the ‚ÄãIndian ‚ÄãOcean, Sri Lanka's ‚Äådeputy ‚ÅÝforeign minister told local ‚Äãtelevision ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝWednesday.

item.title: US kills Iranian leader of Trump assassination plot, Pentagon says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄãU.S. military said on Wednesday that it ‚Äåkilled an Iranian official who headed a unit behind an alleged assassination plot against President Donald Trump but that ‚Äãthe target was not the initial focus ‚Äãof the war.
"The leader of the unit ‚ÅÝwho attempted to assassinate President Trump has been ‚Äãhunted down and killed. Iran tried to kill ‚ÄãPresident Trump and President Trump got the last laugh," Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told a news briefing.
"While that ‚Äãwas not the focus of the effort by ‚Äãany stretch of the imagination - in fact, never raised by ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝPresident or anybody else - I ensured, and others ensured, that those who were responsible for that were eventually part of the target list," ‚ÄãHegseth told reporters.
Hegseth ‚Äãdid not ‚ÅÝname the individual but said the operation took place on Tuesday.
In ‚Äã2024, the U.S. Justice Department charged an Iranian ‚Äãman ‚ÅÝin connection with an alleged plot ordered by the IRGC to assassinate Trump, then U.S. president-elect.
Tehran has ‚ÅÝdenied ‚Äãaccusations that it had targeted ‚ÄãTrump and other U.S. officials.

item.title: Who is Mojtaba Khamenei, frontrunner to be Iran's supreme leader?
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's son Mojtaba has emerged as frontrunner to succeed his late father as Iran's supreme leader after years spent forging close ties with the elite Revolutionary Guards and building influence in the ‚Äãclerical establishment.
Mojtaba Khamenei, 56, has survived the U.S.-Israeli air war on Iran and is seen by Iran's establishment as a potential successor to his father, who was killed in ‚Äåan airstrike on Saturday, two Iranian sources said on Wednesday.
A powerful mid-ranking cleric, Mojtaba has opposed reformers seeking to engage with the West as it tries to curb Iran's nuclear programme, and has long greater freedoms.
His close ties with the Revolutionary Guards (IRGC) give him added leverage across Iran's political and security apparatus and he has built up influence behind the scenes as his father's "gatekeeper", sources familiar with the matter said.
"He has strong constituency and support within the IRGC, in ‚Äãparticular amongst the younger radical generations," said Kasra Aarabi, head of researching the IRGC at United Against Nuclear Iran, a U.S.-based policy organisation.
"So if Mojtaba is alive, there is a ‚Äãhigh chance that he will succeed (his father)," he said, describing Mojtaba as already operating as a "mini supreme leader".
The Assembly of ‚ÅÝExperts that will select the new leader is "close to a conclusion" and will announce its decision soon, Assembly member Ayatollah Ahmad Khatami told state TV on Wednesday, without naming the candidates.
The supreme leader ‚Äãhas the final say on matters of state, including foreign policy and Iran's nuclear programme. Western powers want to prevent Tehran developing nuclear arms. Iran says its nuclear programme is for civilian purposes only.
If elected, Mojtaba ‚Äãwill face pressure from U.S. sanctions that have hammered the economy and could face opposition from Iranians who have shown they are ready to stage mass protests to press their demands for greater freedoms despite bloody crackdowns by the authorities.
Mojtaba was born in 1969 in the city of Mashhad and grew up as his father was helping lead the opposition to the Shah. As a young man, he served in the Iran-Iraq war.
Mojtaba studied under religious conservatives ‚Äãin the seminaries of Qom, Iran's center of Shi'ite theological learning, and has the clerical rank of Hojjatoleslam.
He has never held a formal position in the Islamic Republic's government, despite being widely seen ‚Äãas the gatekeeper to his father. He has appeared at loyalist rallies, but has rarely spoken in public.
His role has long been a source of controversy in Iran, with critics rejecting any hint of dynastic politics in ‚Äåa country that ‚ÅÝoverthrew a U.S.-backed monarch in 1979.
The U.S. Treasury Department imposed sanctions on Mojtaba in 2019, saying he represented the supreme leader in "an official capacity despite never being elected or appointed to a government position" aside from working in his father's office.
Its website said Khamenei had delegated some of his responsibilities to Mojtaba, whom it said had worked closely with the commander of the IRGC's Quds Force and the Basij, a religious militia affiliated with the Guards, "to advance his father‚Äôs destabilising regional ambitions and oppressive domestic objectives".
Mojtaba was a particular target for criticism by protesters during unrest over the death of a young ‚Äãwoman in police custody in 2022, after she ‚Äãwas arrested for allegedly breaching the Islamic Republic's ‚ÅÝstrict dress codes.
In 2024, a video was widely shared in which he announced the suspension of Islamic jurisprudence classes he was teaching at Qom, fuelling speculation about the reasons.
Mojtaba bears a strong resemblance to his father, and wears the black turban of a sayyed, indicating his family traces its lineage to ‚Äãthe Prophet Mohammad.
Critics say Mojtaba lacks the clerical credentials to be supreme leader - Hojjatoleslam is a notch below the rank of Ayatollah, the ‚Äãposition held by his father ‚ÅÝand Ruhollah Khomeini, who founded the Islamic Republic.
But he has remained in the frame, particularly after another leading candidate for the role - the former President Ebrahim Raisi - died in a helicopter crash in 2024.
A U.S. diplomatic cable written in 2007 and published by WikiLeaks cited three Iranian sources describing Mojtaba as an avenue to reach Khamenei.
Mojtaba was widely believed to have been behind the sudden rise of hardliner Mahmoud Ahmadinejad, who was ‚ÅÝelected president in ‚Äã2005.
Mojtaba backed Ahmadinejad in 2009 when he won a second term in a disputed election which resulted in anti-government protests ‚Äãthat were violently suppressed by the Basij and other security forces.
Mehdi Karroubi, a moderate cleric who ran in the election, wrote a letter to Khamenei at the time objecting to what he alleged was Mojtaba's role in supporting Ahmadinejad. Khamenei rejected the ‚Äãaccusation.
Mojtaba's wife, who was killed in Saturday's airstrikes, was the daughter of a prominent hardliner, the former parliament speaker Gholamali Haddadadel.

item.title: US says it is winning against Iran, can fight as long as needed
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. Defense Secretary ‚ÄãPete Hegseth said ‚Äåon Wednesday the United States was ‚Äãwinning in ‚Äãthe war against Iran ‚ÅÝand that ‚Äãthe U.S. military ‚Äãcould fight as long as needed.
"Our air ‚Äãdefenses and ‚Äãthat of our allies ‚Äåhave ‚ÅÝplenty of runway. We can sustain this fight ‚Äãeasily ‚Äãfor ‚ÅÝas long as we ‚Äãneed to," ‚ÄãHegseth ‚ÅÝsaid.

item.title: Iran could disrupt the Strait of Hormuz with drones for months
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Iranian drone attacks could disrupt the Strait of Hormuz for months, but how long the Islamic Republic could sustain its missile barrage is less clear, according to ‚Äãintelligence sources and military analysts.
Since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on Saturday, Iran has launched hundreds of missiles and more than 1,000 drones at Gulf states allied with Washington. ‚ÄåMost were intercepted by air defences, but some residential and commercial buildings, infrastructure and U.S. military bases have sustained damage.
Tehran is a major drone manufacturer and has the industrial capacity to produce around 10,000 per month, according to the Centre for Information Resilience, a non-profit research group funded by Britain's Foreign Office.
The size of its missile stockpile is unknown, with estimates ranging from 2,500 by Israel's military to around 6,000 according to other analysts. How much of Iran's arms ‚Äãcache remains could be a key factor in determining the course of the war.
Closing the Strait of Hormuz , a narrow chokepoint between Iran and Oman through which a fifth of the world‚Äôs ‚Äãcrude oil and liquefied natural gas passes, has been one of Iran's main objectives, and shipping through the crucial energy artery has ground to a near ‚ÅÝhalt after Iranian hits on six vessels.
Energy prices have jumped, with Brent crude rising 12% and a European natural gas benchmark up about 50% so far this week.
"Iran is not going to fold easily ‚Äãor quickly, they have the means to make it unsafe for commercial traffic to flow through Hormuz," said Bob McNally, president of Rapidan Energy Group.
"The U.S. is prioritising attacking Iran‚Äôs munitions, bases and facilities that ‚Äãthreaten the Strait. But all Iran has to do is show they can hit a few tankers and concern will take care of the rest, folks just won‚Äôt go through,‚Äù McNally said.
Strategic missile supplies are a vulnerable point for Iran, according to a former director at Britain‚Äôs MI6 intelligence agency.
‚ÄúRussia is in no position to resupply and China will be quite cautious about this. If it became known that China was actually providing some sort of ‚Äãserious military hardware to Iran, that would play very badly with the GCC states,‚Äù he said, referring to members of the Gulf Cooperation Council comprising Bahrain, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Oman and the United ‚ÄãArab Emirates.
Missile stores may be lower because Tehran had been supplying Hezbollah in Lebanon and the Houthis in Yemen, a second Western intelligence source said.
Stocks were also reduced during a 12-day war with Israel in June, but they ‚Äåhave been partially ‚ÅÝrestored, according to Israeli military intelligence.
A key constraint may be launchers for the missiles. Supplies have at least halved over the last year due to strikes by Israel and the U.S. and further declined over the past five days, research by Britain‚Äôs CIR showed.
Despite this, Iran is likely to be able to sustain a fight with its drones , opens new tab . The country‚Äôs latest generation of Shahed-136 drones have a range of 700 to 1,000 kilometres (435 to 620 miles), sufficient to reach anywhere in the southern Gulf coast when launched from the Iranian mainland or vessels, according to Farzin Nadimi, a senior fellow at the Washington Institute.
Many are produced at ‚Äãdual-use plants and other facilities could be retooled to ‚Äãramp up production, a CIR analyst said.
These ‚ÅÝdrones were able to penetrate Gulf states‚Äô air defence systems, with 65 entering the UAE since the conflict broke out.
They struck Amazon‚Äôs data centres , Dubai International Airport and a Fairmont hotel . Bahrain has suffered material damage to infrastructure, a U.S. naval base and a tower containing a hotel and apartments from drones.
Oil traders are bracing for further price spikes as the length of the disruption to the Strait of Hormuz becomes clearer in the coming days.
‚ÄúI ‚Äãam very concerned, this risk ‚ÅÝis currently underpriced in oil markets,‚Äù said a senior executive at Vitol, a global commodity trading house. "The prevalent theory is that Iran is using old missiles and drones first to deplete air defences. If so, their response is yet to start properly."
And if missiles and drones start to run out, Iran could deploy sea mines. Tehran has an inventory of 5,000 to 6,000 such mines, according to Dryad Global, a maritime risk intelligence firm.
They ‚ÅÝcan be moored ‚Äãto the seabed, rocket-propelled or drift in waters, exploding when a vessel comes into contact. There is no indication that mines ‚Äãhave been laid in the Strait of Hormuz at this point, analysts said.
‚ÄúIf sea mines are laid, it will take a long time to deal with them," said Cormac McCarry, director at Control Risks with a focus on maritime intelligence and security services. "That's ‚Äãwhere we will be looking at months of destruction."

item.title: Mideast crisis highlights risk of dollar liquidity shock
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 4 (Reuters) - Investors have piled into dollars this week amid the turmoil in the Middle East , a reminder that the ongoing transition away from a dollar-centric financial universe toward a more fractured, multi-polar world could be very rocky.
The war now spreading across the region following the joint U.S.-Israeli assault on Iran on Saturday is lifting ‚Äãthe dollar's value, as investors seek the relative safety of the world's most liquid asset.
Equity indices that were major outperformers in the first two months of the year are ‚Äåtanking: South Korea's KOSPI (.KS11) , opens new tab ‚Äì which soared 50% through February ‚Äì has lost nearly 20% in two days. Redemptions from private credit funds are soaring, the dollar spiked as much as 2% in two days, and Treasury yields are shooting higher too.
Matt King, founder of Satori Insights, says this dollar surge isn't the result of a sudden rethink in the growth or inflation outlook. The issue is simply "money flow" - a rapid unwinding of the speculative froth that boosted many markets in recent ‚Äãmonths as investors now scramble for liquidity.
Despite all the fears about dollar debasement, investors in a foxhole still want - and need - dollars.
This raises the wider question of what ‚Äãwill happen in future crises if the long-term erosion of dollar dominance persists.
The dollar's (.DXY) , opens new tab decline as the undisputed leader in global trade, financing, and FX reserves has ‚ÅÝbeen underway for the best part of a quarter century, since the advent of the euro in 1999 and China's accession to the World Trade Organization in 2001.
The U.S. currency's share of global FX ‚Äãreserves has fallen to 57% today from over 70% in the early 2000s, according to the International Monetary Fund.
But because the erosion has been smooth and gradual, dollar liquidity has continued to increase, and the ‚Äãglobal financial system has built buffers against liquidity squeezes after overcoming the historic shocks of 2008 and 2020.
However, the U.S. alliances, rules-based order, and forces of globalization that once ensured dollar liquidity greased the wheels of the world economy and markets, are crumbling. Major trade, political and military conflicts have erupted in the past year, turning the global investment landscape into extremely hazardous terrain.
This is the backdrop against which Barry Eichengreen, University of California, Berkeley professor and renowned expert on international capital flows ‚Äãand currencies, publishes his new book "Money Beyond Borders: Global Currencies from Croesus to Crypto" on March 17.
Eichengreen traces the 2,500-year history of money, explains why systemically important currencies rise to prominence and then fade ‚Äãaway, and offers his assessment of what the future role of the dollar and cryptocurrencies will be.
While he argues that there is still no rival to the dollar as the preeminent currency in FX reserves and international trade, financing, and ‚Äåinvoicing, he ‚ÅÝfears that its decline on these fronts, which has so far been "glacial", could accelerate.
"I'm much more worried than I was in the past," Eichengreen says. "There isn't an obvious alternative to the greenback, and we have to continue to hope that the coming transition will be very gradual and smooth. But I think we're learning that we don't live in a world where things occur smoothly anymore."
The past few days have been anything but smooth, and they highlight how much the world still needs dollars.
The U.S. dollar is on one side of 89% of all FX transactions, according to the Bank for International ‚ÄãSettlements. That's the highest in 25 years. The next ‚Äãmost traded currency is the euro, which ‚ÅÝis on one side of 29% of all FX transactions.
Additionally, the dollar's share of international payments is about 50%. If you include intra‚Äëeuro zone payments in the calculation, that share rises to around 60%, according to a Federal Reserve study. Some 55% of international and foreign currency bank claims, and 60% of liabilities are ‚Äãdenominated in dollars.
Looking at oil, it is estimated that as much as 20% of the world's crude trade is now priced in currencies other than ‚Äãthe dollar, such as the ‚ÅÝeuro or Chinese yuan. Of course, that means around 80% is still denominated in dollars.
Eichengreen says he has long believed that a multi-polar global monetary and financial system would be good for the world, just as a more diverse ecosystem is good for the planet.
"But we're not yet at a point where other sources of global liquidity could step up and substitute for the dollar. So we are at a very delicate point in ‚ÅÝtime," Eichengreen ‚Äãsays.
At a time when trade wars and real wars are flaring up, that seems something of an understatement.
(The opinions expressed here ‚Äãare those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the ‚Äãbiggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Hormuz reopening is not solely in Trump‚Äôs gift
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Donald Trump is waking up to the fact that cutting off the world's largest oil and gas shipping route has dire consequences. That's clear from his scramble to restart flows through the Strait of Hormuz ‚Äãby offering insurance , opens new tab for tankers and dispatching U.S. Navy escorts. The idea evokes the late-1980s ‚ÄúTanker War‚Äù, when Washington protected oil shipments during the Iran-Iraq ‚ÄåWar. But this time a quick fix is unlikely.
Traders rely on the narrow channel at the end of the Arabian Gulf to transport a fifth of the world‚Äôs oil and gas. Just days after an audacious attack on Iran that effectively halted trade flows, Trump rushed to reassure them of U.S. help. Firstly, he is offering to underwrite risks that private insurers no longer ‚Äãwant to bear via the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation (DFC). The entity is authorised to provide $1 billion in political risk insurance per entity, well ‚Äãabove the value of a typical new tanker costing up to $120 million, per Evercore analysts. It currently has a total ‚ÅÝmaximum liability of $205 billion.
In theory, such coverage could coax reluctant tanker owners back into the Gulf. In practice, the opposite looks likely. Freight rates for very large ‚Äãcrude carriers have nearly doubled since January, while many war-risk insurers are pulling back entirely. A government guarantee might cover financial losses, but it does little to counter ‚Äãthreats of drones, sea mines and Iranian-led forces. A subsidiary of China‚Äôs state-owned COSCO Shipping, hours after the DFC announcement, suspended , opens new tab all new booking services for relevant routes.
Operational snags loom as well. The DFC's insurance product may still require lengthy approvals, Evercore reckons. Tanker operators, meanwhile, must decide now whether it is worth the danger.
And even if financial cover materialised quickly, military protection ‚Äãmay be even trickier to pull off. During the 1980s Iran-Iraq war, the U.S. led escort operations but had a larger fleet and could present itself as ‚Äãa semi-neutral guardian of shipping lanes. Today the U.S. Navy has fewer available assets, according to AXSMarine analysts, while Washington is a direct participant in the confrontation. That makes escorted ‚Äåtankers more ‚ÅÝlikely to become targets.
The strategic backdrop is even less forgiving. Attacks on export infrastructure in the United Arab Emirates have shown even backup routes are vulnerable. Saudi Aramco is exploring alternatives through the Red Sea, but that route itself faced threats and harassments from Iran-aligned Houthi militants and has been effectively closed since 2024. In fact, the risk is so high that container shipowners stayed away even after Trump brought the Houthis to a peace deal last May and they have only recently ‚Äãstated their intention to return.
If the chokepoint ‚Äãstays disrupted, oil prices will rocket ‚ÅÝfurther. A full closure without any offsets could remove roughly 20 million barrels a day from global supply, Goldman Sachs analysts reckon. Apply Goldman‚Äôs rule of thumb that every million barrels per day of lost oil supply for a year adds $8 ‚Äãto oil prices, based on data since 1998. If the Hormuz disruption lasts three months, that formula suggests crude ‚Äãcould jump $40 per barrel. ‚ÅÝIn that scenario, $100 oil would become the new normal.
Follow Yawen Chen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran crisis jumbles the Gulf state pecking order
item.description:
LONDON, March 2 (Reuters Breakingviews) - The six countries of the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) have entered a new and disturbing world. Amid sustained Iranian rocket and drone attacks on most members of the group ‚Äì which includes Bahrain, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Oman ‚Äãand the United Arab Emirates ‚Äì Western investors and tourists may now cool on a region that saw foreign direct investment more than quadruple to $83 ‚Äåbillion in the decade to 2024. At the very least, a reshuffling of the pecking order in Saudi's favour seems possible.
With UAE airspace closed at the weekend, Saudi probably looks immediately more appealing to expats and foreign financiers, who have flocked en masse to the Gulf for years. Some companies have been driving staff across the border , opens new tab to Riyadh, where flights out of the region have remained in ‚Äãoperation.
Global banks like JPMorgan, HSBC and Morgan Stanley arguably have financial reasons to move staff to the Saudi capital and away from the UAE, not least ‚Äãthe promise of more advisory mandates from state-linked corporate giants like the Public Investment Fund. But bankers still find Dubai more ‚ÅÝlivable, partly because alcohol is available. Even though local defences have downed most of the 689 drones thrown at the UAE, the shocking spectacle of the Fairmont Hotel in ‚ÄãDubai catching fire on Saturday may change attitudes.
The bigger strategic issue worrying Western corporations with a big presence in the Gulf, according to a person familiar with one firm's ‚Äãthinking, is what sort of Iran emerges next. A scenario referenced by U.S. President Donald Trump, in which Iranians rise up to overthrow the Islamic Republic and embrace democracy, is one possibility. But corporate leaders are well aware that an Iran that remains in the grip of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) could be an even greater threat than the status quo.
Thus far, GCC states have ‚Äãlargely restricted themselves to a joint statement , opens new tab on Sunday, which highlighted their right to defend themselves. But the news on Monday that QatarEnergy had been forced by drone attacks ‚Äãto halt production may now shift Gulf countries further towards a military response ‚Äì Qatar itself said later in the day it had shot down two Iranian aircraft. The risk of sustained conflict ‚Äåacross the ‚ÅÝregion might also further complicate the movement of fossil fuel shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, which carries a fifth of the world's daily oil consumption.
It‚Äôs hard to disentangle where this would leave the relative economic health of Saudi and the UAE, who are comfortably the GCC‚Äôs biggest economies and who have of late been in a well-publicised spat . JPMorgan analysts on Monday cut estimates for 2026 UAE non-oil GDP growth by 0.5 percentage points, while only reducing Saudi‚Äôs by 0.3 percentage points. Though the Emirati economy is starting ‚Äãfrom a healthier fiscal position, Riyadh has more ‚Äãscope to move oil exports by ‚ÅÝpipeline to avoid the disrupted Gulf. Equally, the energy sectors of both economies would benefit from higher prices .
The big picture, however, is that the UAE has outshone Saudi in attracting foreign money in the past few years, as reflected in an inflated property market. If ‚Äãit continues to suffer Iranian attacks, that trend may not continue.
Follow George Hay on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Turkey has prepared plans for possible migrant flow from Iran, minister says
item.description:
ISTANBUL, March 4 (Reuters) - Turkey has drawn up plans to deal with a potential inflow of people fleeing ‚Äãthe war in neighbouring Iran , Interior Minister Mustafa ‚ÄåCiftci said on Wednesday, with preparations including possible buffer zones along the frontier and tent camps.
There was currently ‚Äãno unusual movement at the three border ‚Äãgates along the Iran-Turkey border, Ciftci added.
Speaking in ‚ÅÝAnkara, Ciftci said three contingency plans had ‚Äãbeen drawn up by authorities: managing any potential migrant ‚Äãflow on the Iranian side of the border; creating buffer zones along the frontier if movement cannot be stopped; ‚Äãand letting people into Turkey under controlled conditions.
He ‚Äãadded that Turkey had prepared initial capacity to host up ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝ90,000 people in the event of a sudden inflow, including tent camps and temporary accommodation sites.
On Monday, a Reuters witness saw hundreds of Iranians crossing ‚Äãthe border into ‚ÄãTurkey. Others ‚ÅÝwere reportedly waiting to cross. Ciftci said authorities had been informed that ‚ÄãIran was restricting its own citizens from ‚Äãleaving ‚ÅÝthe country, while allowing Turkish nationals and third-country citizens to exit.
According to data provided by the ‚ÅÝminister, some ‚Äã5,010 people entered Turkey between ‚ÄãMarch 1 and 3, while 5,495 people exited.

item.title: New Zealand deploys two defence planes to the Middle East in preparation for evacuations
item.description:
DUBAI, March 4 (Reuters) - The New Zealand government said on Wednesday that it would send two Defence Force C-130J ‚ÄãHercules aircraft to the Middle East in case ‚Äåthey were needed for evacuating New Zealanders from the region.
New Zealanders are among the tens of thousands of travellers across ‚Äãthe Middle East, who have become stranded after escalating ‚Äãconflict between the U.S., Israel and Iran disrupted flights ‚ÅÝworldwide and forced the closure of major airports including ‚ÄãDubai, the world's busiest international hub.
The government said there ‚Äãwere currently around 3000 New Zealanders registered as living in the Middle East.
‚ÄúThe events in the Middle East are fast-moving, dangerous ‚Äãand complex - and our primary focus is on ‚Äãassisting New Zealanders in the region to the extent possible in ‚Äåthis ‚ÅÝvery challenging security environment,‚Äù Foreign Minister Winston Peters said in a statement.
He added that sending two planes along with consular staff was so "they can be ready when ‚Äãconditions allow to ‚Äãassist with ‚ÅÝany civilian evacuation operations."
Peters added that with airspace closed and most commercial flights ‚Äãnot operating, the government continues to recommend ‚Äãthat New ‚ÅÝZealanders shelter in place - or take any safe and practical opportunities that are available to leave the region.
The ‚ÅÝstatement ‚Äãsaid that decisions on where the ‚Äãconsular response team and the two Hercules would be deployed were still ‚Äãbeing made.

item.title: Milei appoints chief Buenos Aires prosecutor as new justice minister
item.description:
BUENOS AIRES, March 4 (Reuters) - Argentine President Javier Milei said on Wednesday he has appointed chief ‚ÄãBuenos Aires prosecutor Juan Bautista Mahiques ‚Äåas the country's new justice minister.
Mahiques, who has been the attorney general of the Argentine capital since ‚ÄãOctober 2019, will replace Mariano Cuneo ‚ÄãLibarona, who had been justice minister ‚ÅÝsince December 2023, Milei announced in a posting on X.
Local ‚Äãnewspaper La Nacion reported that Libarona had ‚Äãcited personal reasons for his departure.
"I assume this role with the conviction that without legal certainty there ‚Äãis no investment, without stable rules ‚Äãthere is no development, and without independent judges there ‚Äåis ‚ÅÝno republic," Mahiques said in a separate post on X.
In a speech before Congress last Sunday, Milei announced that this year he will ‚Äãseek to ‚Äãpass more ‚ÅÝreforms to the penal code to introduce harsher sentences for criminals.
The government ‚Äãwon several legislative victories last ‚Äãweek ‚ÅÝwhen Congress approved a controversial labor reform and a change to the penal code that lowers the ‚ÅÝage ‚Äãof criminal responsibility from ‚Äã16 to 14.

item.title: China to send envoy to Middle East for mediation, foreign minister says
item.description:
BEIJING, March 4 (Reuters) - China will send a special envoy to the ‚ÄãMiddle East for mediation, Foreign ‚ÄåMinister Wang Yi told his Saudi Arabian and UAE counterparts on ‚ÄãWednesday according to statements ‚Äãfrom his ministry, as conflict ‚ÅÝin the region continued to ‚Äãescalate.
China appreciates Saudi Arabia's restraint ‚Äãand insistence on resolving differences through peaceful means, Wang was quoted as ‚Äãsaying in a phone call ‚Äãwith the Saudi minister by the Chinese ‚Äåministry.
In ‚ÅÝa separate phone call with the foreign minister of the United Arab Emirates, Wang ‚Äãsaid the "red ‚Äãline" ‚ÅÝof protecting civilians in conflicts must not be ‚Äãcrossed, and that non-military ‚Äãtargets, ‚ÅÝincluding those involving energy, should not be attacked. He also ‚ÅÝcalled ‚Äãfor protecting the ‚Äãsafety of shipping routes.

item.title: Exclusive: US carried out strike on Iranian warship off Sri Lanka coast, officials say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - The U.S. military carried out a strike ‚Äãthat sank an Iranian warship off Sri Lanka's coast and triggered ‚Äåa rescue for dozens of Iranian sailors by Sri Lanka's navy, three U.S. officials told Reuters on Wednesday.
One of the officials, ‚Äãwho spoke on condition of anonymity due to the ‚Äãsensitivity of the issue, said the strike was carried ‚ÅÝout by a U.S. military submarine.
The U.S. strike ‚Äãtriggered a distress call and the Sri Lankan armed forces ‚Äãlaunched a search and rescue operation. Sri Lankan authorities said they had rescued 32 people so far and recovered several bodies from the ‚Äãsea.
Sources in Sri Lanka's navy and defence ministry said ‚Äãthe vessel had been attacked by a submarine and at least ‚Äå101 ‚ÅÝpeople were missing in the incident that took place off Sri Lanka's Indian Ocean coast.
The defence sources said it was unclear who attacked the ship.
A Sri Lankan navy ‚Äãspokesman said the ‚Äãreport of ‚ÅÝ101 people being missing was not true and rejected any reports on the cause ‚Äãof the ship sinking.
U.S. strikes on Iran's ‚ÄãNavy is ‚ÅÝa central part of the war against Iran, launch by the United States on Saturday.
On Tuesday, the U.S. military's ‚ÅÝCentral ‚ÄãCommand said it had already sunk ‚Äã17 Iranian warships and was on track to destroy Iran's entire navy.

item.title: Malta-flagged container ship hit by projectile in Hormuz, vessel abandoned, sources say
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - The Maltese-flagged container ship Safeen Prestige was damaged ‚Äãby a projectile as it ‚Äåsailed towards the top of the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday, prompting ‚Äãits crew to abandon ‚Äãship, shipping sources said.
The vessel was ‚ÅÝhit some two nautical miles ‚Äãnorth of Oman while transiting ‚Äãeastbound in the Strait of Hormuz at 1109 GMT, British maritime risk management ‚Äãcompany Vanguard said.
"The vessel was ‚Äãstruck by an unknown projectile just above ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝwaterline, resulting in a fire in the engine room. No environmental impact has been reported ‚Äãat this ‚Äãtime," Vanguard ‚ÅÝsaid.
British navy agency UKMTO reported it had received ‚Äãa report involving a ‚Äãunnamed ‚ÅÝcontainer ship being hit by an unknown projectile and the crew ‚ÅÝabandoned ‚Äãthe vessel with ‚Äãno reported injuries.

item.title: Top Vatican cardinal calls US-Israeli strikes 'truly alarming'
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 4 (Reuters) - The Vatican's top diplomat warned on Wednesday that the ongoing U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran undermine international law and ‚Äãsaid nations do not have a right to launch "preventive wars", ‚Äåin an unusually direct criticism of the military campaign.
"If states were to be recognised as having a right to 'preventive war' ‚Ä¶ the entire world could risk going ‚Äãup in flames," Cardinal Pietro Parolin, the Vatican's Secretary of ‚ÄãState, said in an interview with Vatican News.
Asked about ‚ÅÝthe U.S.-Israeli strikes, which have pressed on for the fifth day, ‚ÄãParolin said they had caused a "weakening of international law (that) is truly alarming".
"The ‚Äãrule of force has replaced the force of law, with the conviction that peace can arise only after the enemy has been annihilated," the cardinal told ‚Äãthe Vatican's media outlet.
It is unusual for Vatican diplomats to openly ‚Äãcritique specific military campaigns. Vatican officials usually prefer to avoid press coverage and operate ‚Äåbehind ‚ÅÝthe scenes, leaving open the possibility of the Church serving as mediator in conflicts.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said the strikes against Iran were needed to prevent the country from developing a nuclear weapon, ‚Äãwhich Tehran denies ‚Äãseeking, and to ‚ÅÝthwart its long-range ballistic missile program.
Parolin, the Vatican's lead diplomat since 2013 and a front-runner in the ‚Äã2025 conclave of cardinals that eventually elected Pope Leo, ‚Äãis known ‚ÅÝas someone who is usually very cautious with words.
Leo did not address the ongoing conflict in his weekly audience on Wednesday with pilgrims ‚ÅÝin St. ‚ÄãPeter's Square.
The pope made an impassioned ‚Äãappeal on Sunday for an end to the conflict. He called for a stop what ‚Äãhe termed a "spiral of violence".

item.title: Chinese Communist Party elder Song Ping dies aged 109
item.description:
BEIJING, March 4 - Chinese Communist Party elder and the longest-lived member of ‚Äãthe elite Politburo Standing Committee, ‚ÄåSong Ping, died on Wednesday at the age of 109, the official Xinhua ‚Äãnews agency reported.
Song died in ‚ÄãBeijing due to illness, according ‚ÅÝto Xinhua.
Born in 1917, Song was ‚Äãat the core of the ‚Äãcommunist party's second generation of leadership, an era when reform-minded Deng Xiaoping remade China ‚Äãafter the 27-year rule of ‚ÄãMao Zedong.
Song was credited with bringing Hu Jintao, ‚ÄåChina's ‚ÅÝleader before current President Xi Jinping, to the attention of Deng.
Song was in the Politburo Standing Committee, ‚Äãthe ‚Äãapex of ‚ÅÝpower in China's political system, from 1989-1992.
Earlier, he ‚Äãhad worked in key party ‚Äãand ‚ÅÝgovernment posts.
He had worked as a personal secretary of Zhou Enlai, who ‚ÅÝserved ‚Äãas communist China's ‚Äãfirst premier from 1949 until his death in ‚Äã1976.

item.title: Hungary foreign minister hopes Russia will free two ethnic Hungarian prisoners of war during talks
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 4 (Reuters) - Hungary's Foreign Minister Peter Szijjarto said on Wednesday that he hopes two ethnic Hungarian prisoners of war will be freed ‚Äãby Russia during his talks in Moscow, where he is scheduled to meet Russian ‚ÄåPresident Vladimir Putin later.
Szijjarto travelled to Moscow a day after Hungary's Prime Minister Viktor Orban held a phone call with Putin where they discussed the situation in the ‚ÄãMiddle East, Ukraine and the availability of crude oil and natural ‚Äãgas for Hungary.
Orban's government has made Russia's war on ‚ÅÝUkraine a key topic in his campaign for the April 12 parliamentary election, ‚Äãadding to tensions between Budapest and Kyiv.
Szijjarto said in a broadcast ‚Äãon his Facebook page from Moscow that two ethnic Hungarian prisoners of war have recently asked Hungary for help.
"I hope that after our talks more people will fly ‚Äãhome on the plane than who came in this direction," Szijjarto said.
‚ÄãUkraine is home to around 150,000 ethnic Hungarians, most of them in the Transcarpathia ‚Äåregion. ‚ÅÝOrban's government and Kyiv have long clashed over the community's language rights.
Orban's government has also accused Kyiv of conscripting ethnic Hungarians, who Budapest said should not have been called up. Last Friday the foreign minister summoned Kyiv's ‚Äãambassador to Budapest ‚Äãto protest the ‚ÅÝconscription of two men.
Hungary's Orban has maintained warm relations with Moscow even after the start of the war ‚Äãin Ukraine, and is not willing to give up ‚Äãits purchases ‚ÅÝof Russian oil and gas, which has led to tensions with the European Union. Last month Hungary said that it will block the European Union's next ‚ÅÝpackage of ‚Äãsanctions against Russia as well as a ‚Äã90 billion euro ($105 billion) EU loan for Ukraine to fund its defence against Russia until ‚Äãshipments on the Druzhba pipeline resume.

item.title: US extends flight restrictions to Haiti capital until early September
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - The U.S. Federal Aviation Administration said on ‚ÄãThursday it was extending its prohibition on U.S. ‚Äåflights landing in Haitian capital Port-au-Prince through September 3, citing risks from armed groups to civil aviation.
In November 2024, the FAA halted ‚Äãall flights to Haiti after three U.S. ‚Äãcommercial jetliners were struck by gunfire. It later ‚ÅÝallowed flights to resume to six other airports in ‚Äãnorthern Haiti, but kept the ban on Port-au-Prince.
That restriction ‚Äãhad been set to expire on Saturday, but the FAA said on Thursday it was extending the bar in response to ‚Äãsecurity forces' continued inability to prevent attacks against aircraft ‚Äãin Port-au-Prince and surrounding regions.
U.S. planes can transit over Port-au-Prince above ‚Äå10,000 ‚ÅÝfeet (3,048 m).
Armed gangs control much of the Haitian capital amid a political limbo, with no president in place or elections scheduled and a U.N.-backed expanded security force pending .
Since ‚ÄãSeptember 2025, ‚ÄãHaitian groups have ‚ÅÝused small-arms fire to attack at least three aircraft in the area where ‚ÄãU.S. civil aviation is prohibited, according to ‚Äãthe FAA.
The ‚ÅÝFAA said an "expanded and shifted gang operating area raises the risk from small arms fire to civil aviation ‚ÅÝoperations ‚Äãat lower altitudes."
The agency continues to ‚Äãallow flights to six airports in Haiti: Port-de-Paix, Cap-Haitien, Pignon, Jeremie, ‚ÄãAntoine-Simon and Jacmel.

item.title: Russian LNG tanker sinks in Mediterranean, Moscow blames Ukrainian drones
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 4 (Reuters) - A Russian tanker carrying liquefied natural gas (LNG) sank in ‚Äåthe Mediterranean on Wednesday after what Moscow described as an attack by Ukrainian drones launched from Libya.
The Arctic Metagaz sank in waters between Libya and Malta after catching fire a ‚Äãday earlier, Libya's maritime rescue agency said in an advisory seen ‚Äãby Reuters.
"Information indicates that the tanker experienced sudden explosions followed ‚ÅÝby a massive fire, which ultimately led to its complete sinking," the ‚Äãagency said.
Ukraine has frequently targeted Russian oil refineries and other energy infrastructure in ‚Äãan attempt to deprive Russia's war machine of funding, but if confirmed, this would be its first attack on an LNG tanker.
The Security Service of Ukraine did not respond ‚Äãto a Reuters request for comment.
Russia's transport ministry said the tanker, which ‚Äãhad been carrying LNG from the Arctic port of Murmansk, was attacked by Ukrainian naval drones ‚Äålaunched ‚ÅÝfrom the coast of Libya.
It said the 30 crew members, all Russian nationals, were safe, and thanked Maltese rescue services.
"We qualify what happened as an act of international terrorism and maritime piracy, a gross violation of the fundamental ‚Äãnorms of international maritime ‚Äãlaw," the ‚ÅÝministry said.
Libya's National Oil Corporation said it had no involvement with the tanker, adding that it was en route ‚Äãto Port Said, Egypt.
The ship's Russia-based manager, LLC SMP Techmanagement, ‚Äãand Russian ‚ÅÝLNG producer Novatek (NVTK.MM) , opens new tab have not commented on the incident.
