U.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äãhas also criticised Starmer for failing to provide sufficient support for his strikes on Iran.
Starmer told a press conference Britain ‚Äãhad the right plan and had already ‚Äãbeen pre-deploying military equipment across the region before the start ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝthe war.
"My focus is providing calm, level headed leadership in the national interest," Starmer said.
"That means deploying our military and diplomatic strength to protect our ‚Äãpeople, and ‚Äãit means ‚ÅÝhaving the strength to stand firm by our values and our principles, ‚Äãno matter the pressure to do otherwise."
He ‚Äãsaid ‚ÅÝBritain was sending four additional typhoon jets to join the squadron in Qatar to strengthen our defensive operations there and ‚ÅÝin ‚Äãthe region.
"We have the right ‚Äãplan for defence," he said.

item.title: Israel orders residents to leave southern Beirut
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 5 (Reuters) - Israel warned residents to leave Beirut's Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs on Thursday, sparking panic as people fled a swathe of the Lebanese capital on the fourth day of full-scale ‚Äãhostilities between the Iran-backed group and Israel.
An Israeli military spokesperson, in ‚Äãa post on X, ordered residents of the southern suburbs ‚ÅÝto move east and north, posting a map showing four sprawling districts of ‚Äãthe capital he said they must leave, part of the area is adjacent ‚Äãto Beirut airport.
"Save your lives, evacuate your homes immediately," Israeli military spokesperson Avichay Adraee posted, saying that any movement southwards may endanger their lives.
Roads out of the suburbs were clogged ‚Äãas people fled by car and on foot, television footage showed. Gunfire ‚Äãwas heard in the southern suburbs, warning residents to leave.
Lebanon was pulled into the war in ‚Äåthe Middle ‚ÅÝEast on Monday, when Hezbollah opened fire, sparking intensified Israeli airstrikes largely focused on the southern suburbs, southern Lebanon and eastern Lebanon.
On Wednesday, the Israeli military ordered residents to leave an area of southern Lebanon amounting to about 8% of its ‚Äãterritory.
Israeli bombardment and warnings ‚Äãhave already forced ‚ÅÝtens of thousands of Lebanese to flee homes in the southern suburbs and the south this week. The Lebanese ‚Äãhealth ministry has said 77 people have been killed.
There ‚Äãhave been ‚ÅÝno reports of fatalities in Israel as a result of Hezbollah attacks.
Beirut's predominantly Shi'ite Muslim southern suburbs are among the capital's most densely populated areas. The ‚ÅÝarea ‚Äãwas pounded by Israeli airstrikes during a war ‚Äãwith Hezbollah in 2024, and during a previous war with Israel in 2006.

item.title: Algerian military aircraft crashes after takeoff from Boufarik base, two crew killed
item.description: March 5 (Reuters) - A small ‚ÄãAlgerian military ‚Äåtransport aircraft ‚Äãcrashed ‚Äãshortly after taking ‚ÅÝoff ‚Äãfrom Boufarik ‚Äãair base on ‚ÄãThursday, ‚Äãkilling two crew ‚Äåmembers ‚ÅÝand injuring four others, ‚ÄãEnnahar ‚ÄãTV ‚ÅÝreported ‚Äãciting the ‚Äãdefence ‚ÅÝministry.

item.title: US opposes IAEA board resolution condemning attacks on Ukraine's power grid
item.description:
VIENNA, March 5 (Reuters) - The United States joined Russia, China, and ‚ÄåNiger on Thursday in opposing a resolution adopted by the U.N. nuclear watchdog's board denouncing attacks on Ukraine's energy infrastructure as a threat to nuclear safety, diplomats said.
The resolution, passed by the ‚ÄãInternational Atomic Energy Agency's 35-nation Board of Governors, is the seventh on Ukraine ‚Äãsince Russia invaded its neighbour four years ago.
This is the first time ‚ÅÝthe United States has opposed one.
"While we continue supporting the IAEA's work in-country, ‚Äãwe do not support the Board's current consideration of an unnecessary resolution that does not ‚Äãhelp achieve peace between Ukraine and Russia," the United States said in its statement to the board before the vote.
U.S. President Donald Trump has in the past year pressured Ukraine for a quick peace ‚Äãdeal that could involve ceding land to Russia. Ukraine has ruled out giving up ‚Äãterritory.
The IAEA board passed the resolution with 20 votes in favour, including France, Britain, Australia, Canada, South ‚ÄåAfrica ‚ÅÝand Argentina, 10 abstentions and the four votes against, diplomats at the closed-door meeting said. Brazil, Egypt, Morocco and Saudi Arabia were among the abstentions.
The resolution's wording was not as strong as previous ones.
The text seen by Reuters said the board "reemphasizes that attacks ‚Äãtargeting Ukraine's energy infrastructure ‚Äãfor the off-site power ‚ÅÝsupply of nuclear power plants, including at the ZNPP (Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant), represent a direct threat to nuclear safety and security".
The ‚Äãmove follows the United States' abstention at the U.N. General Assembly on ‚Äãthe anniversary ‚ÅÝof the invasion in February when it passed a resolution supporting Ukraine, backing its international borders and voicing concern over intensifying Russian attacks on civilians and critical energy infrastructure.
That resolution ‚ÅÝpassed ‚Äãwith 107 countries voting in favour, 12 against, and ‚Äã51 abstentions.
The United States said at the time that the General Assembly resolution included language likely to distract ‚Äãfrom ongoing negotiations aimed at ending the conflict.

item.title: Cartels can only be defeated with military power, White House's Miller says
item.description:
MIAMI, March 5 (Reuters) - Cartels operating in the ‚ÄãWestern Hemisphere can ‚Äåonly be defeated with military power, ‚ÄãWhite House ‚ÄãDeputy Chief of Staff ‚ÅÝStephen Miller ‚Äãsaid on Thursday.
"We ‚Äãhave learned after decades of effort is that ‚Äãthere is ‚Äãnot a criminal justice ‚Äåsolution ‚ÅÝto the cartel problem," Miller told Latin American ‚Äãdefense leaders ‚Äãgathered ‚ÅÝat the U.S. Southern ‚ÄãCommand headquarters ‚Äãfor ‚ÅÝa conference.

item.title: Greek seafarers strike over crews stranded in the Gulf by Iran war
item.description:
PIRAEUS, Greece, March 5 (Reuters) - Greek seafarers began a 24-hour strike on Thursday, halting local ferry services, as they protested over vessel crews stranded in the Gulf amid the escalating ‚ÄãMiddle East war, and demanded the area is declared a war risk ‚Äåzone to enable their repatriation.
The Iran conflict threatens Gulf ports and has already disrupted global trade through the Strait of Hormuz, a key artery accounting for about 20% of global oil and gas supply. ‚ÄãAlthough the Strait is not shut, Iran has warned that it will fire ‚Äãon any ship trying to pass through.
Greece is a dominant force in ‚ÅÝglobal shipping, controlling one of the world‚Äôs largest merchant fleets. More than 325 ships of ‚ÄãGreek interests, their crews including dozens of Greek seafarers, are in the wider Gulf ‚Äãarea.
"We demand that all of our colleagues, currently in the dangerous Gulf area, the Gulf of Oman and the Red Sea, are evacuated and safely repatriated," said Angelos Galanopoulos, head of Greece's lower engine ‚Äãcrews' union Stephenson.
Dozens of protesters rallied outside the shipowners union near the port of ‚ÄãPiraeus and spray-painted: "No sacrifice for profits and wars" on the ground. A motorcycle convoy drove to ‚Äåthe shipping ‚ÅÝministry.
The International Maritime Organization says it is concerned for about roughly 20,000 seafarers in the region. At least nine ships have been damaged in strikes since the conflict began on Saturday.
Seafarers usually have the contractual right to refuse to sail in designated war zones ‚Äãand demand repatriation at ‚Äãthe shipowner's expense.
Dozens ‚ÅÝof ships remained at anchor in open waters off the coast of major Gulf producers and dozens of oil tankers were inside the ‚ÄãStrait, ship tracking data showed.
Risks include not only attacks but also ‚Äãpotential food ‚ÅÝand supply shortages said Apostolis Kypraios, head of the marine engineers' union PEMEN.
"The government and shipownwers are responsible for the people trapped in war areas," Kypraios said.
"We demand that they ‚ÅÝfind ‚Äãa solution for our colleagues to return home. Their ‚Äãfamilies are worried and seafarers don't know if they will return alive, if they will be injured."

item.title: As Iran conflict rages, Pope Leo asks God to help leaders renounce war
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 5 (Reuters) - Pope Leo released a video on Thursday praying that God would help world leaders renounce war ‚Äãas a means of resolving conflicts in an unusual ‚Äåappeal as the U.S.-Israeli bombing campaign against Iran pressed on for the sixth day.
"Lord, enlighten the leaders of the nations, so they may have the courage ‚Äãto abandon projects of death," the pontiff said in the ‚Äãvideo message.
"Today we lift up our prayer for peace ‚ÅÝin the world, asking that nations renounce weapons and choose the ‚Äãpath of dialogue and diplomacy," he said.
Leo releases a video message ‚Äãeach month to announce his prayer intentions for that month. The pope's intention for March is "for disarmament and peace".
It was unclear if Thursday's video was ‚Äãcreated specifically to respond to the U.S.-Israeli military campaign, which has ‚Äãset off a regional war with Iranian attacks in Israel, the Gulf and ‚ÄåIraq, ‚ÅÝand Israeli attacks in Lebanon.
The Vatican did not immediately respond to a question about when the video was recorded.
The Vatican's top diplomat warned on Wednesday that the U.S.-Israeli strikes undermined international law and said ‚Äãnations did not ‚Äãhave a ‚ÅÝright to launch "preventive wars", an unusually direct criticism of the military campaign.
"If states were to be recognised ‚Äãas having a right to 'preventive war' ... the entire ‚Äãworld could ‚ÅÝrisk going up in flames," Cardinal Pietro Parolin, the Vatican's secretary of state, said in an interview with Vatican News.
In his video, the ‚ÅÝpope ‚Äãasked God to help the world ‚Äãunderstand "that true security does not come from control fuelled by fear but from trust, justice ‚Äãand solidarity among peoples."

item.title: Diplomats in Riyadh ordered to shelter in place due to potential threat, sources say
item.description:
RIYADH, March 5 (Reuters) - Diplomats and staff at ‚Äåembassies in Riyadh's Diplomatic Quarter were told to shelter ‚Äãin place on ‚ÄãThursday due to a potential ‚ÅÝbut unspecified threat, ‚Äãfour people with direct ‚Äãknowledge of the matter said.
They did not have further details, ‚Äãand the Saudi government ‚Äãmedia office did not immediately ‚Äårespond ‚ÅÝto a request for comment.
The gates to the Diplomatic Quarter, a neighborhood on ‚Äãthe western ‚Äãedge ‚ÅÝof the Saudi capital that houses ‚Äãmost foreign missions ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝthe country, were closed as of Thursday afternoon, ‚ÅÝtwo ‚Äãof the people ‚Äãsaid.

item.title: Exclusive: NATO's Rutte 'not deaf' to criticism of his Trump praise, offers more
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte said on Thursday he was "not ‚Äãdeaf" to criticism that he goes overboard in praising Donald Trump but argued the U.S. president ‚Äådeserved it.
However, in an interview with Reuters , Rutte also offered praise for Spain - a target of Trump's ire for refusing to allow U.S. military bases on its soil to be used in the American-Israeli assault on Iran .
Rutte, a Dutch former prime minister, was selected to lead NATO partly ‚Äãdue to his strong bond with Trump , which diplomats say has helped keep the military alliance together despite the ‚ÄãU.S. president's criticism of the organisation and its members.
But some officials, analysts and commentators have ‚ÅÝaccused Rutte of going over the top in lavishing praise on Trump, arguing it is demeaning to Europe and ‚Äãdownplays the continent's ability to defend itself.
"I hear the criticism, obviously. I'm not deaf," Rutte said in the interview at ‚ÄãNATO headquarters in Brussels.
Famously, at a NATO summit, Rutte likened Trump to a "daddy" intervening in last year's conflict between Israel and Iran.
Most recently, he has offered strong backing for the new U.S.-Israeli military action against Iran, even though leaders of ‚Äãsome NATO nations such as Spain and Turkey have been critical of the intervention and some others have not endorsed ‚Äãit.
In the interview, Rutte said Trump had played a crucial role in getting NATO allies to agree a new spending target of ‚Äå5% ‚ÅÝof GDP last year, composed of 3.5% on core defence and 1.5% on broader security-related spending.
The previous NATO target was to spend 2% of GDP on core defence.
Rutte also called the U.S.-Israeli assault on Iran "decisive action to take out this capability of Iran to pose a threat as an exporter of terrorism and chaos to the Middle East, to Europe, ‚Äãto the world".
"I ‚ÅÝthink if a president of a country is ... providing that type of leadership, some praise is warranted," he said.
On Tuesday, Trump threatened to impose a full U.S. trade embargo on ‚ÄãSpain due to the row over the military bases and also renewed criticism of ‚ÄãMadrid for not ‚ÅÝsigning up to the 5% defence spending target.
Asked about the spat between Trump and Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez , Rutte offered praise for Spain's contribution to NATO.
"Their troops are part of many foreign land forces, many initiatives, many NATO missions. I really want ‚ÅÝto commend ‚Äãthem for that," he said.
In response to a question about whether ‚Äãhe had spoken to Trump and Sanchez to lower the tension between them, Rutte said: "When there are debates between allies, I always try to stay ‚Äãa bit muted, and therefore being able, if necessary, to help a bit."

item.title: Zelenskiy taunts Hungary's Orban for blocking aid to Ukraine
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy taunted Hungarian leader Viktor Orban ‚Äåon Thursday for blocking a 90 billion euro ($104 billion) aid package from the European Union critical to Kyiv's survival as it fends off Russia.
Ukraine relies on financial aid from partners to cover its budgetary needs while it funnels most state funds ‚Äãtoward defence. EU member Hungary, which has maintained cordial ties with Moscow, vetoed the package this ‚Äãmonth amid a dispute over oil supplies.
"We hope a certain person in the EU will ‚ÅÝnot keep blocking the 90 billion... and Ukrainian soldiers will have weapons," Zelenskiy told reporters in Kyiv.
"Otherwise, we ‚Äãwill give the address of this person to our armed forces, our guys. Let them call him, speak ‚Äãwith him in their own language."
The remark will likely heighten the tension between the two leaders during Russia's four-year war on Ukraine, which Orban - who faces elections on April 12 - has been careful not to condemn.
Hungary's veto of the aid package, ‚Äãas well as new EU sanctions on Russia, came in response to what it says was Ukraine deliberately ‚Äãcutting supplies from the Druzhba pipeline carrying Russian crude to Europe.
Kyiv says the oil flows stopped after a Russian attack ‚Äåon ‚ÅÝpipeline infrastructure in January and it is fixing the damage as fast as it can. Zelenskiy said on Thursday that the Soviet-era pipeline could be operational within a month and a half.
"They (the Russians) are killing us, and we're supposed to give poor little Orban oil, because without it he won't win elections?" he said.
Orban has ‚Äãmade the Ukraine war ‚Äãa focal point in his ‚ÅÝcampaign for re-election.
Ukraine's troops are fending off grinding Russian assaults along several parts of the 1,200-kilometer front line, as Kyiv faces U.S. pressure to secure peace while ‚Äãfending off Russian demands to cede land.
Despite being outgunned and outmanned, Kyiv's army ‚Äãhas retaken territory ‚ÅÝin recent weeks, gaining more in February than it lost for the first time since 2023, according to the Finland-based Black Bird Group analysis team.
European foreign ministers had tried unsuccessfully this month to persuade Budapest not to punish ‚ÅÝUkraine ‚Äãfor delays restarting the pipeline, which also supplies Slovakia, another EU ‚Äãneighbour of Ukraine.
Slovakia, whose Prime Minister Robert Fico is also sympathetic to the Kremlin, said it would refuse requests from Kyiv for emergency ‚Äãelectricity supplies until oil flows resume via the Druzhba pipeline.
($1 = 0.8625 euros)

item.title: Exclusive: Trump tells Reuters US should have a role in choosing Iran's next leader, encourages Kurdish opposition
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told Reuters on Thursday that he wants the United States to play a role in ‚Äãchoosing Iran's next leader and encouraged Kurdish opposition groups to go on the ‚Äåoffensive.
Trump said in a telephone interview it was very early in the process of picking a new leader but that the late Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's son Mojtaba was an unlikely choice, without elaborating.
"We want ‚Äãto be involved in the process of choosing the person who is going ‚Äãto lead Iran into the future," Trump said. "We don't have to go ‚ÅÝback every five years and do this again and again."
Trump also encouraged Iranian Kurdish forces to go ‚Äãon the offensive.
"I think it's wonderful that they want to do that, I'd be all ‚Äãfor it," the president said.
When asked if the U.S. would provide or had offered air cover, he responded, "I can't tell you that," but added that the objective for the Kurds would be "to win."
"If they're ‚Äãgoing to do that, that's good," Trump added.
Iranian Kurdish militias have consulted with the United ‚ÄãStates in recent days about whether, and how, to attack Iran's security forces in the western part ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝthe country, according to three sources with knowledge of the matter.
The Iranian Kurdish coalition of groups based on the Iran-Iraq border in the semi-autonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan has been training to mount such an attack in hopes of weakening the country's military, as the ‚ÄãUnited States and Israel ‚Äãpound Iranian targets ‚ÅÝwith bombs and missiles.
Trump also signaled confidence that the major shipping route near Iran, the Strait of Hormuz, will remain open.
Closing the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz , a narrow chokepoint between Iran and Oman through which ‚Äãa fifth of ‚ÅÝthe world‚Äôs crude oil and liquefied natural gas passes, has been one of Iran's main objectives, and shipping through the crucial energy artery has ground to a near halt after Iranian ‚ÅÝhits ‚Äãon six vessels.
"They have no navy, you know the ‚Äãnavy is now at the bottom of the sea," Trump said. "I'm watching Hormuz very closely."

item.title: Isolated and under fire: Iran strikes out as Russia and China stand aside
item.description:
DUBAI, March 5 (Reuters) - With its supreme leader killed and its war machine under relentless U.S. pressure , Iran now stands largely alone - its longtime partners Russia and China offering nothing more than diplomatic condemnations and expressions of concern.
Tehran has responded to the U.S. ‚Äãand Israeli attacks by widening the conflict beyond the Middle East, firing missiles and drones with an impact that is reverberating through global energy markets , rattling capitals from Washington to Beijing, and ‚Äåparalyzing the shipping that carries 20% of the world's oil supply through the Strait of Hormuz.
Iranian missiles reached as far as Cyprus, Azerbaijan , Turkey and Gulf states, taking the war to their doorstep by targeting critical businesses, energy infrastructure and U.S. bases. Oil facilities, refineries and key supply routes were hit, causing severe disruption to crude and natural gas supplies.
With the Strait of Hormuz already shut, the attacks have sent energy prices soaring, destabilising global markets and forcing major economies to scramble , underscoring the world‚Äôs exposure to the ‚Äãfallout from Tehran‚Äôs response to the war.
Russia and China‚Äôs restraint reflects a cold calculation, analysts say: intervening as Iran faces Israel and the United States would bring high costs, limited gains and unpredictable ‚Äãrisks - burdens neither power appears willing to shoulder.
‚ÄúPutin has other priorities, and chief among them is Ukraine,‚Äù said Anna Borshchevskaya, a Russia expert at the Washington Institute. ‚ÄúIt ‚ÅÝwould be foolish for Russia to go into a direct military confrontation with the United States.‚Äù
A senior Russian source said ‚Äúthe escalation in and around Iran and the Gulf is already diverting attention from the war in Ukraine. That‚Äôs ‚Äãjust a fact. Everything else is just emotion about a ‚Äòfallen ally‚Äô,‚Äù the source said.
Beijing and Moscow have both helped Iran build military capacity to counter U.S. and Israeli pressure, supplying missiles, air‚Äëdefence systems and technology intended to ‚Äãbolster deterrence, complicate U.S. operations and raise the costs of attack. That support, however, now appears capped.
China has spent years embedding itself in Middle Eastern diplomacy, while Russia has cast Iran as a pillar of its anti-Western alignment.
Yet as the conflict flared, both powers were constrained -China by its dependence on Gulf energy and trade and by security priorities in Asia, and Russia by a grinding war in Ukraine that has sapped its capacity to shield partners and sharpened its need to preserve ties with ‚Äãoil-rich Gulf states.
The result is a stark paradox: Iran remains strategically useful to both, but not useful enough to fight for.
With Russia‚Äôs military, diplomatic bandwidth and economic resources still absorbed by the war in Ukraine, President Vladimir ‚ÄãPutin‚Äôs priority is to avoid escalation with Washington and safeguard Russia‚Äôs interests in the Middle East, rather than gamble on Iran‚Äôs battlefield fortunes.
‚ÄúIf Russia had supported Iran directly, it would have alienated the Gulf states and Israel,‚Äù Borshchevskaya said. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs not ‚Äåwhat Putin wants."
Beijing‚Äôs ‚ÅÝrestrained response reflects a longstanding strategy: avoiding binding security commitments far from its core interests.
Unlike the United States, whose alliances rest on mutual defence obligations, China prefers partnerships built on trade, investment and arms sales, ties that stop short of dragging it into costly conflicts beyond East Asia, said Evan A. Feigenbaum from the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace.
Beijing, one of the world‚Äôs largest trading powers and energy buyers, maintains ties with Iran and Gulf Sunni rivals, and in Latin America, never placed all its bets on Venezuela alone.
"If Beijing wanted to do more, it won‚Äôt redirect strategic attention or military assets from core security," argues Henry Tugendhat ‚Äãat the Washington Institute. "It only cares about its name ‚Äãabroad. It cares about Taiwan, the South China ‚ÅÝSea, and perceived threats from the U.S. and Japan.‚Äù
The conflict may even carry advantages for Beijing. From the sidelines, China can watch as U.S. forces are tied down far from East Asia and military stockpiles are depleted, while gaining a real-time view of American capabilities and operations, insights that could inform its thinking on a future ‚ÄãTaiwan scenario.
China‚Äôs key vulnerability remains energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz, which carries some 45% of its oil imports. But Beijing has built strategic reserves ‚Äãand substantial volumes of Iranian oil ‚ÅÝalready sitting in tankers or storage, experts say.
They say the crisis has allowed Moscow and Beijing to recast themselves as mediators. China said Foreign Minister Wang Yi has spoken with European and Arab ministers to press for dialogue, while Putin has held similar calls with Gulf leaders and Iranian officials.
Russia also sees concrete benefits: rising oil prices strengthen its war economy, and a U.S. administration tied up in the Middle East has less bandwidth for ‚ÅÝUkraine.
Russia does not ‚Äãbenefit from the collapse of the Iranian regime, but it is also not tying its fate to Tehran‚Äôs survival, said Borshchevskaya. Moscow ‚Äãis hedging, preserving flexibility, regardless of the conflict‚Äôs outcome, and would build ties with any new government, even one aligned with Washington.
The Russian source pointed to Syria as a precedent. Despite backing ousted president Bashar al‚ÄëAssad for years, Moscow retained its Mediterranean bases and quickly ‚Äãbuilt ties with Syria‚Äôs new leader, Ahmed al‚ÄëSharaa, underscoring its willingness to trade loyalty for long-term leverage.

item.title: Trump says he needs to be involved in selecting Iran's next leader, Axios reports
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told Axios ‚Äãon Thursday that he needs to ‚Äåbe personally involved in selecting Iran's next leader.
"Khamenei's son is unacceptable to me. ‚ÄãWe want someone that will ‚Äãbring harmony and peace to Iran," ‚ÅÝAxios quoted Trump as saying ‚Äãin an interview.
"I have to be ‚Äãinvolved in the appointment, like with Delcy (Rodriguez) in Venezuela," Trump said.
Mojtaba Khamenei, son of ‚ÄãIran's late Supreme Leader, has survived ‚Äãthe U.S. and Israeli airstrikes on Iran ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝwhich his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed, Iranian sources told Reuters on Wednesday.
A mid-ranking cleric with close ties ‚Äãto Iran's ‚Äãelite ‚ÅÝRevolutionary Guards, hardliner Mojtaba is one of the most ‚Äãinfluential figures in the Iranian ‚Äãclerical ‚ÅÝestablishment and is seen as a possible successor to his father.
Iran has ‚ÅÝnot ‚Äãyet announced a new ‚Äãleader.

item.title: Trump renews Netanyahu pardon call, saying only Iran should be troubling him
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday called again on Israel's president to grant Prime Minister Benjamin ‚ÄãNetanyahu a pardon for corruption charges, saying the premier should ‚Äåhave nothing on his mind but the war with Iran.
KEY CONTEXT
WHAT'S NEXT

item.title: Swiss and EU vow to deepen cooperation on security and defence
item.description:
ZURICH, March 5 (Reuters) - Switzerland and the European Union on Thursday said they planned to deepen ‚Äãcooperation on security and defence, including exchange ‚Äåof information in areas ranging from Ukraine to the Middle East.
The two sides issued a joint ‚Äãdeclaration stating that aim following talks in ‚ÄãZurich between Swiss Foreign Minister Ignazio Cassis ‚ÅÝand EU foreign policy chief Kaja ‚ÄãKallas.
A draft of the declaration said the two ‚Äãsides' top diplomats aimed to hold annual dialogue and step up exchanges on issues and places of ‚Äãjoint interest including Ukraine, the Middle East, ‚ÄãAfrica, Asia, Latin America and the United States.
The two sides ‚Äåpledged ‚ÅÝto strengthen cooperation on foreign policy, security and defence, and Swiss representatives would be invited, as appropriate, to meetings of the ‚ÄãEU Foreign Affairs ‚ÄãCouncil and ‚ÅÝrelevant preparatory bodies, it said.
Issues of common interest also encompassed human ‚Äãrights, peace mediation, multilateral affairs, restrictive ‚Äãmeasures, ‚ÅÝconsular affairs, and crisis management abroad.
The declaration said the two planned to boost cooperation between ‚ÅÝEU ‚Äãdelegations and offices and ‚ÄãSwiss embassies and missions in third countries and to international ‚Äãorganisations.

item.title: France ramps up military aid to Lebanon as Macron calls on Hezbollah, Israel to halt attacks
item.description:
PARIS, March 5 (Reuters) - France will strengthen its cooperation with the Lebanese Armed ‚ÄãForces and provide armored transport vehicles as well ‚Äåas operational and logistical support, French President Emmanuel Macron said on Thursday, as Lebanon was pulled deeper into the war in ‚Äãthe Middle East earlier this week.
"Everything must be ‚Äãdone to prevent this country, so close to ‚ÅÝFrance, from once again being drawn into war," Macron ‚Äãsaid in a post on X.
"At this moment of ‚Äãgreat danger, I call on the Israeli prime minister not to expand the war to Lebanon. I call on Iranian leaders not ‚Äãto further draw Lebanon into a war that ‚Äãis not its own," the French leader added.
After meeting with the ‚Äåchief ‚ÅÝof staff of the French military in Beirut, Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun asked Macron to intervene to "prevent the targeting of the southern suburbs following threats by the Israeli ‚Äãarmy against ‚Äãits residents," the ‚ÅÝLebanese presidency said in a separate statement.
France has said that it aims to ‚Äãprevent escalation across the region and has taken steps ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝprotect its own positions amid the wider conflict involving the United States, Israel and Iran .
"Hezbollah must immediately cease its fire ‚ÅÝtoward ‚ÄãIsrael. Israel must refrain from ‚Äãany ground intervention or large-scale operation on Lebanese territory," Macron said.

item.title: Flights for US citizens stuck in Middle East ramping up, State Dept says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. State Department said on Thursday it was ramping up the provision of charter flights from the Middle East ‚Äãafter criticism over the Trump administration's planning and initial assistance to ‚ÄåU.S. citizens trying to leave the region since the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran began.
The strikes that began on Saturday and Iran's retaliatory attacks on its neighbors have led to widespread ‚Äãairspace closures, clogging busy aviation routes throughout the region and leaving ‚Äãthousands stranded.
The department, headed by Secretary of State Marco Rubio, announced ‚ÅÝon Wednesday the first U.S. government charter flight departed the region bound ‚Äãfor the U.S., giving few details.
"At the direction of @SecRubio, Department of State charter ‚Äãflight and ground transportation operations are underway and will continue to ramp up with additional flights and ground transports taking place today," Assistant Secretary of State for Global Public ‚ÄãAffairs Dylan Johnson said in a post on X on Thursday.
It was ‚Äãunclear from which countries charter flights would be departing.
American citizens in Kuwait, Bahrain, the United ‚ÄåArab ‚ÅÝEmirates, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and Israel should fill out a crisis intake form to receive information about upcoming flights and ground transportation options, Johnson said, adding that a new task force has helped more than 10,000 Americans with ‚Äãguidance since the crisis ‚Äãbegan.
The State Department ‚ÅÝhad only issued warnings to U.S. citizens in Israel and Lebanon as tensions rose, but Iran's response has targeted ‚ÄãU.S. missions and civilian infrastructure in many more places, ‚Äãincluding in ‚ÅÝGulf Arab nations that are major transport hubs.
U.S. security alerts for Americans in Kuwait, the United Arab Emirates, Iraq, Qatar and Bahrain went out after the conflict ‚ÅÝbegan. ‚ÄãThe U.S. on Monday urged Americans across 14 ‚Äãcountries in the Middle East to immediately depart the region using "available commercial transportation" without offering any U.S.-government-vouched ‚Äãmeans, drawing the ire of U.S. lawmakers .

item.title: Ukraine, Russia exchange 200 POWs each in latest swap
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - Ukraine and Russia exchanged 200 prisoners of war each on Thursday in their latest swap, ‚Äãofficials on both sides said, despite a ‚Äådeadlock in U.S.-backed negotiations to end the four-year conflict .
Kyiv and Moscow have carried out regular POW exchanges throughout the ‚Äãwar, committing during recent talks in Geneva to ‚Äãa new round of such swaps even ‚ÅÝas negotiations remain stuck over the question of ‚Äãterritory.
"Every time our people come home, it proves ‚Äãthat Ukraine is working to bring everyone back. No one is forgotten," President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on X.
"We involve mediators. ‚ÄãI am grateful to everyone who helps Ukraine. ‚ÄãI thank the United States for its support in making ‚Äåthis ‚ÅÝexchange possible."
Some of the POWs freed on Thursday were captured as far back as Moscow's siege of Mariupol in early 2022, Zelenskiy added.
Tearful ‚Äãrelatives greeted loved ‚Äãones - many ‚ÅÝof them draped in Ukrainian flags - as they emerged from a ‚Äãbus that had arrived at an undisclosed ‚Äãlocation ‚ÅÝto chants of "Welcome!"
The two sides are expected to trade a further 300 POWs each in a ‚ÅÝsecond ‚Äãday of exchanges on Friday, ‚Äãofficials said.

item.title: Finland to lift full ban on hosting nuclear arms, government says
item.description:
HELSINKI, March 5 (Reuters) - Finland plans to lift a long-standing ban on having nuclear arms on its territory, the government said on Thursday, aligning with Nordic neighbours ‚Äãin a move that could open the door to deploying atomic ‚Äåbombs on Finnish soil during times of war.
Finland's Nuclear Energy Act, passed in 1987, prohibits the import, manufacture, possession and detonation of nuclear explosives on its soil, seen by some ‚ÄãFinns as a clause that would benefit only Russia if there ‚Äãever was a war.
While Finland maintained neutrality during the Cold War ‚ÅÝera, the country in 2023 joined the NATO military alliance in response ‚Äãto nuclear-armed Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine the preceding year.
"The amendment is necessary ‚Äãto enable Finland's military defense as part of the alliance and to take full advantage of NATO's deterrence and collective defence," Finnish Defence Minister Antti Hakkanen told a press conference.
The proposed ‚Äãchange will next go to parliament where the right-wing coalition government holds ‚Äãa majority.
Neighbours Sweden, Denmark and Norway have long-standing policies against nuclear weapons on their territories in ‚Äåpeacetime ‚ÅÝbut do not have legislative bans during war.
NATO allies France and Germany announced plans on Monday to deepen cooperation with European partners on nuclear deterrence, marking a policy shift as the continent faces rising threats from Russia and instability linked ‚Äãto the Iran conflict .
Sweden's ‚Äãdoctrine is to ‚ÅÝstation no permanent foreign troops or nuclear weapons on its soil in peacetime, Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson told reporters last ‚Äãweek, when asked about the possibility of his country hosting French ‚Äãnuclear arms .
"If ‚ÅÝwe were to find ourselves in a completely different situation, that particular formulation would not apply," Kristersson said.
Finland shares a 1,340-kilometre border with Russia and in 2024 ‚ÅÝsigned ‚Äãa defence pact with the United States, allowing ‚Äãit to use 15 of Finland's military facilities and zones.

item.title: Middle East chaos stems from erosion in international law, EU's Kallas says
item.description:
ZURICH, March 5 (Reuters) - The current turmoil in the Middle East is a direct ‚Äãresult of the erosion in international law, EU foreign ‚Äåpolicy chief Kaja Kallas said on Thursday.
In a speech at the University of Zurich, Kallas said the international order has been undermined by major powers ‚Äãacting unilaterally, pointing the finger particularly at Russia, but also ‚Äãdirecting criticism at China and the United States.
"Today, the ‚ÅÝchaos we see around us in the Middle East is ‚Äãa direct consequence of the erosion of international law," she said, ‚Äãarguing that Russia's invasion of Ukraine had encouraged others to act with impunity.
China, she said, was also taking advantage of the erosion of international rules to ‚Äãextend its influence in the Asia-Pacific region and put pressure ‚Äãon Europe's economies.
"Without restoring international law, together with accountability, we are doomed to ‚Äåsee ‚ÅÝrepeated violations of the law, disruption and chaos," Kallas argued.
Turning to the United States, she said Washington's foreign policy shift had "rocked the transatlantic relationship to its foundation, with aftershocks in other parts of ‚Äãthe world," and ‚Äãcalling its impact ‚ÅÝon the international order "seismic."
"The current direction is a new world order characterised by competition and coercive ‚Äãpower politics, a world order dominated by a handful ‚Äãof ‚ÅÝmilitary powers who aim to establish and secure spheres of influence," Kallas said.
Kallas was delivering the Churchill Special Lecture some 80 years after ‚ÅÝBritain's ‚Äãwartime leader Winston Churchill spoke at the ‚ÄãUniversity and called for the building of a "United States of Europe" following the devastation ‚Äãof World War Two.

item.title: Seafarers can refuse to sail through Mideast Gulf region, main union says
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - Seafarers have the right to refuse to sail on ships passing ‚Äãthrough the Middle East Gulf, including the Strait ‚Äåof Hormuz, after the threat level for the region was raised to its highest level, the leading labour union and shipping industry groups ‚Äãsaid on Thursday.
Around 300 ships are anchored on ‚Äãboth sides of the Strait while the U.S.-led war against ‚ÅÝIran escalates . Since February 28, nine ships have been ‚Äãdamaged and at least one seafarer has been killed.
As ‚Äãpart of negotiated arrangements reached on Thursday between mariners and commercial shipping companies, known as the International Bargaining Forum (IBF), seafarers can refuse to ‚Äãsail into the area, with repatriation at the company‚Äôs cost ‚Äãand compensation equal to two months‚Äô basic wage.
In addition, they will ‚Äåreceive ‚ÅÝhigher pay and compensation will be doubled in the event of death or disability, the International Transport Workers' Federation (ITF) said in a statement.
"Today‚Äôs designation ensures that seafarers on vessels ‚Äãcovered by IBF ‚Äãagreements have ‚ÅÝcritical protections if they operate in this dangerous region," ITF General Secretary Stephen Cotton said.
"The ‚Äãfact we have to take these measures at ‚Äãall ‚ÅÝis a damning indictment of the situation facing seafarers today. No worker should have to risk being killed or maimed simply ‚ÅÝfor ‚Äãdoing their job ‚Äì particularly when that ‚Äãjob is transporting the oil and goods that keep the world‚Äôs economies running."
